Encourage natural enemies (Encarsia wasp).
Prune out infested wood during dormancy.
Spot treatment with insecticidal soap on light infestations.
entries.prevention: Dormant oil annual schedule. Inspect new trees before planting.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard peach OFM. Redhaven is the standard early-mid peach in the US; OFM pressure is consistent and predictable.
organicManagement: Pheromone mating disruption.
Spinosad timed by trap monitoring.
Granulosis virus products.
Sanitation of dropped fruit.
entries.prevention: Pheromone trap network. Remove pruning debris from orchard.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard stone fruit brown rot. Elberta is highly susceptible; the heritage California peach has been displaced commercially partly due to brown rot vulnerability in humid eastern climates.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at 7-day intervals during pre-harvest weeks.
Harvest fruit dry; brown rot enters through bruises and skin wounds.
Remove all mummified fruit and twig cankers during dormancy.
Cool storage immediately after harvest.
entries.prevention: Reconsider Elberta planting in humid eastern climates. Open canopy pruning. Avoid overhead irrigation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as peach OFM: shoot flagging then fruit damage. Nectarines are sometimes more susceptible than peaches due to thinner skin allowing easier larval entry.
organicManagement: Pheromone mating disruption.
Spinosad timed by trap monitoring.
Granulosis virus products.
Kaolin clay during peak adult flights.
entries.prevention: Pheromone trap network. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as peach borer: gummy frass at trunk base, larval tunnels through cambium. Nectarines share full peach susceptibility.
organicManagement: White latex trunk paint.
Trunk wrap or aluminum foil during oviposition.
Beneficial nematodes drenched at trunk base.
Pheromone trap network.
entries.prevention: Same as peach: clean weed-free trunk zone, monitoring, hand-knife backyard removal.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as peach brown rot. Nectarines are particularly susceptible due to thin skin; brown rot can wipe out a crop in 48 hours during humid pre-harvest weather.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at 7-day intervals from pit-hardening through harvest.
Harvest at first ripeness, handle gently.
Cool storage immediately.
Remove all mummies and twig cankers during dormancy.
entries.prevention: Open canopy pruning. Avoid overhead irrigation. Cultivar selection matters; Fantasia is moderately susceptible.

entries.symptoms: Small fly resembling a fruit fly with banded wings; larvae tunnel through fruit causing brown trails and premature softening. Single generation per year matches the cherry harvest window precisely.
organicManagement: Hang yellow sticky traps with ammonia bait at first sign of ripening (June).
Kaolin clay sprays through the pre-harvest weeks.
Spinosad bait sprays (GF-120, OMRI) target adults.
Net trees with fine-mesh exclusion netting from ripening through harvest.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring identifies first flight. Remove and destroy dropped fruit; pupae overwinter in soil.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center; OSU Extension

entries.symptoms: Black aphids cluster on new shoot tips and leaf undersides; severe infestations cause leaf curl, sticky honeydew, and sooty mold. Heavy infestations stunt young trees.
organicManagement: Strong water spray dislodges colonies.
Insecticidal soap or horticultural oil applied at first sight.
Encourage ladybug and lacewing populations through diverse plantings.
Prune out heavily infested shoots.
entries.prevention: Maintain diverse flowering ground covers for predator habitat. Avoid excess nitrogen which encourages aphid colonies.

entries.symptoms: Small purple-brown circular leaf spots; leaves yellow and drop prematurely starting in midsummer. Severe defoliation weakens trees and reduces winter hardiness.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at petal fall, 2 weeks later, and post-harvest.
Annual fall leaf cleanup.
Prune for open canopy.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
entries.prevention: Fall leaf sanitation is the highest-leverage practice. Open canopy pruning supports rapid drying after rain.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Michigan State Extension

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as sweet cherry fruit fly. Sour cherry's earlier ripening sometimes escapes the heaviest fly flight window in northern regions.
organicManagement: Yellow sticky traps from ripening through harvest.
Kaolin clay pre-harvest.
Spinosad bait sprays.
Exclusion netting on backyard scale.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring. Drop fruit removal.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as sweet cherry leaf spot. Sour cherry is even more susceptible than sweet; severe defoliation can occur within weeks of first symptoms.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at petal fall, 2 weeks later, and at 7-day intervals through summer.
Annual fall leaf cleanup.
Open canopy pruning.
entries.prevention: Site selection: good air drainage. Cultivar selection where possible; some sour cherry cultivars carry partial resistance.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Michigan State Extension

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard sweet cherry fruit fly. Bing's heavy commercial cropping makes precise trap monitoring critical for organic management.
organicManagement: Yellow sticky traps through ripening.
Kaolin clay or spinosad bait sprays.
Exclusion netting for backyard scale.
Drop fruit sanitation.
entries.prevention: Trap-based monitoring. Sanitation through pupae overwintering site management.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

entries.symptoms: Female SWD lays eggs in ripening fruit through serrated ovipositor; larvae feed inside fruit causing rapid softening and rot. Rainier's blush color and thin skin make it exceptionally vulnerable.
organicManagement: Fine-mesh exclusion netting (less than 1mm openings) over trees from color change through harvest.
Spinosad sprays at 7-day intervals during ripening.
Cold storage immediately after harvest; refrigeration kills any eggs and small larvae.
Cup or vinegar traps for monitoring (not effective control alone).
entries.prevention: Harvest fruit at first ripeness; do not leave overripe fruit on trees. Exclusion netting is the most reliable control.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard cherry fruit fly. Rainier's yellow-blush color is exceptionally attractive to adult flies for oviposition.
organicManagement: Combine with SWD exclusion netting for dual coverage.
Yellow sticky traps with ammonia bait.
Kaolin clay pre-harvest.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring. Drop fruit removal.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as sour cherry leaf spot. Montmorency is the standard US tart cherry and is highly susceptible; defoliation by mid-summer is common in unmanaged trees.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at petal fall, 2 weeks later, and post-harvest, plus during summer at 14-day intervals.
Annual fall leaf cleanup.
Open canopy pruning.
entries.prevention: Site selection: well-drained soil with strong air movement. Fall leaf sanitation is mandatory for Montmorency production.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as peach plum curculio: crescent-shaped oviposition scars at petal fall, internal larvae causing drop, surviving fruit shows crescent scar at harvest. Plum is the namesake host.
organicManagement: Kaolin clay from petal fall through 4-6 weeks post-bloom.
Tarp-and-shake at petal fall and 5 days later.
Trap trees with no spray at orchard edges.
Drop fruit sanitation weekly.
entries.prevention: Tarp-and-shake monitoring. Maintain orchard floor cleanliness through June drop.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

entries.symptoms: Aphids cluster on shoot tips and underside of new leaves, causing tight downward leaf curl. Heavy infestations stunt shoot growth. Mealy plum aphid is the related species in the western US.
organicManagement: Strong water spray dislodges colonies.
Dormant oil or insecticidal soap at delayed dormant stage.
Encourage ladybugs and lacewings.
Prune out heavily infested shoots in summer.
entries.prevention: Maintain predator habitat. Avoid excess nitrogen.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as stone-fruit brown rot. European plum (prune-type) is moderately susceptible; the dense fruit clusters trap humidity and amplify infection spread.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at 7-day intervals through pre-harvest weeks.
Open canopy pruning.
Mummified fruit removal during dormancy.
Cool storage after harvest.
entries.prevention: Open canopy. Avoid overhead irrigation. Thin fruit clusters to reduce humidity trapping.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as European plum curculio. Japanese plums are particularly attractive to curculio adults; pressure is generally higher than on European types.
organicManagement: Kaolin clay from petal fall through 4-6 weeks post-bloom.
Tarp-and-shake on cool overcast mornings.
Drop fruit sanitation.
Edge trap trees.
entries.prevention: Monitor with tarp-and-shake at petal fall. Sanitation through June drop.

entries.symptoms: Yellow ring patterns on leaves; deformed fruit with darker ring spots and unsightly bumps. Eventually kills affected trees. Aphid-vectored; once-eradicated in Pennsylvania but a continuing quarantine concern.
organicManagement: There is no organic cure; sanitation by removal is the only effective response.
Remove and destroy infected trees immediately; do not move plant material out of the area.
Report suspected infections to state department of agriculture.
Plant only certified virus-tested nursery stock.
entries.prevention: Source from certified nurseries. Aphid management reduces vector spread. Quarantine zones in affected regions enforce removal of infected trees.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as European plum brown rot. Italian (prune) plum's dense pubescent skin is somewhat more resistant than thin-skinned Japanese plums but still vulnerable in humid pre-harvest weather.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays through pre-harvest.
Open canopy pruning.
Mummy removal during dormancy.
entries.prevention: Open canopy. Avoid overhead irrigation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as Japanese plum curculio. Santa Rosa's early ripening sometimes escapes the worst curculio pressure but is heavily affected in eastern US plantings.
organicManagement: Kaolin clay from petal fall.
Tarp-and-shake.
Drop fruit sanitation.
entries.prevention: Tarp-and-shake at petal fall. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as stone-fruit brown rot. Apricot is among the most susceptible stone fruits; entire crops can be lost in 48 hours during humid pre-harvest weather.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at petal fall and pre-harvest at 7-day intervals.
Open canopy pruning.
Harvest at first ripeness.
Cool storage immediately.
entries.prevention: Reconsider apricot in humid eastern climates without mediterranean-style summers. Cultivar selection for partial resistance.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as plum curculio on plum or peach. Apricot is highly susceptible; the early bloom timing means curculio adults find apricot first in mixed orchards.
organicManagement: Kaolin clay from petal fall through 4-6 weeks post-bloom.
Tarp-and-shake on cool overcast mornings.
Drop fruit sanitation.
entries.prevention: Tarp-and-shake monitoring. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as peach bacterial spot: small dark angular lesions on leaves, fruit cracking and corky pitting. Apricot is moderately susceptible; cool wet springs favor infection.
organicManagement: Copper sprays during fall dormancy and at silver-tip.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
Plant resistant cultivars (Harlayne, Hargrand) where bacterial spot is common.
Prune for canopy openness.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Site for air drainage. Avoid sprinkler irrigation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as peach twig borer: shoot flagging in spring then fruit damage at midsummer. Apricot is a host for the same borer species.
organicManagement: Bt sprays during young larval stage.
Spinosad timed by trap monitoring.
Prune flagged shoots.
entries.prevention: Pheromone trap network. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Adult fly resembles apple maggot but slightly smaller; larvae tunnel through berries causing soft brown spots and premature drop. Single generation per year matched to berry ripening.
organicManagement: Yellow sticky traps with ammonia bait from late June onward.
Spinosad bait sprays during peak adult flight.
Fine-mesh exclusion netting from first color.
Drop berry removal.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring for first flight detection. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Sudden wilting and death of individual canes; brown discoloration extends from a basal infection point. Fungal pathogens (Botryosphaeria) enter through pruning wounds. Most damaging on stressed plants.
organicManagement: Prune out infected canes 6 inches below visible browning during dry weather.
Sterilize pruning tools between cuts.
Maintain consistent soil moisture; drought stress amplifies infection.
Avoid winter injury through proper mulching.
entries.prevention: Plant in well-drained acidic soil. Maintain steady moisture. Avoid wound entry by pruning in dry weather.

entries.symptoms: Adult beetle girdles cane just below the tip in summer, causing visible wilt within hours. Larvae overwinter in cane and tunnel down through the stem the following season.
organicManagement: Prune out girdled cane tips immediately below the girdle marks; destroy.
Spinosad on adult beetles during peak summer flight.
Encourage parasitic wasp populations.
Maintain weed-free row clean of nearby wild brambles.
entries.prevention: Inspect canes weekly during summer for the characteristic shepherd's-crook wilted tip.

entries.symptoms: Clearwing moth lays eggs at cane base; larvae tunnel through cane crown and lower stem, killing canes in their second year. Look for sawdust frass at the crown and brown bored entry points.
organicManagement: Beneficial nematodes drenched at the crown in late summer.
Hand-knife removal of larvae from frass deposits.
Prune out and destroy infested canes during dormancy.
Mulch heavily to discourage adult oviposition near the crown.
entries.prevention: Beneficial nematodes annually. Maintain weed-free crown zone.

entries.symptoms: Purple-rimmed gray cane lesions; cane splits at lesion centers in summer. Severe infections cause cane wilt and reduced fruit production. Most damaging in humid sites.
organicManagement: Lime sulfur dormant spray.
Prune out infected canes during dormancy.
Open row spacing for airflow.
Annual leaf and debris cleanup.
entries.prevention: Site selection: well-drained, well-ventilated rows. Cultivar selection where possible.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as SWD on raspberry. Blackberry is exceptionally vulnerable; the thorny canopy makes exclusion netting management more complex.
organicManagement: Exclusion netting on trellises.
Spinosad at 7-day intervals during ripening.
Cold storage immediately.
Harvest every 1-2 days.
entries.prevention: Netting. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on raspberry: shepherd's crook wilted cane tips from summer girdling. Blackberry is a secondary but consistent host.
organicManagement: Prune out girdled tips below girdle marks.
Spinosad on adult beetles during peak flight.
Maintain weed-free perimeter clean of wild brambles.
entries.prevention: Weekly summer inspection. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Systemic infection causing bright orange spore masses on leaf undersides in spring; infected plants are weakened, produce few fruit, and never recover. Affects blackberry and black raspberry; red raspberry is immune.
organicManagement: Remove and destroy infected plants entirely (including roots); orange rust is systemic and cannot be cured.
Replant with rust-free certified stock from disease-free nurseries.
Maintain distance (more than 100 ft) from wild brambles.
Lime sulfur dormant sprays do not cure infected plants but help limit nearby spread.
entries.prevention: Source certified disease-free stock. Avoid planting near wild brambles. Inspect new plantings in spring for early symptoms.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as other powdery mildews: white powdery coating on leaves and shoots. Gooseberry is particularly susceptible; some cultivars (Pixwell, Hinnomaki Red) carry resistance.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at 7-day intervals during active growth.
Open bush pruning for airflow.
Plant resistant cultivars.
Potassium bicarbonate burn-down on established infections.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Site for air drainage. Avoid overhead irrigation.

entries.symptoms: Green-bodied sawfly larvae feed in groups on leaves; rapid skeletonization of foliage in late spring. Two generations per year; severe infestations defoliate bushes.
organicManagement: Hand-pick larvae from leaf undersides early in feeding.
Spinosad sprays during active feeding.
Strong water spray dislodges larvae.
Encourage parasitic wasp populations.
entries.prevention: Inspect bushes weekly during May and June. Maintain diverse predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Yellow-orange spore pustules on leaf undersides in summer; minor damage to currant itself, but alternates to white pines where it is a lethal disease. Banned or restricted in many regions for white pine protection.
organicManagement: Plant rust-immune cultivars (Crusader, Coronet, Welleck) where currant cultivation is allowed.
Maintain distance (more than 900 ft) from any white pine (Pinus strobus).
Comply with local quarantine laws; some states prohibit currant cultivation outright.
Remove infected leaves and improve airflow.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection for rust immunity. Compliance with local restrictions.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as red currant rust. Black currant (Ribes nigrum) is the most aggressive host; many states prohibit cultivation entirely. Immune cultivars (Consort, Crusader, Titania) are required where allowed.
organicManagement: Plant only certified rust-immune black currant cultivars.
Comply with local cultivation laws (some states still prohibit).
Maintain over 900 ft from any white pine.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Legal compliance.

entries.symptoms: Beetle larvae bore through elder canes; affected canes wilt and die within a season. Look for entrance holes with sawdust frass at branch crotches.
organicManagement: Prune out and destroy affected canes during dormancy.
Encourage parasitic wasp and ground beetle populations.
Beneficial nematodes drenched at the crown in late summer.
Maintain healthy plant vigor through proper irrigation.
entries.prevention: Annual sanitation pruning. Predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as SWD on other thin-skinned berries. Elderberry's tight cluster habit traps humidity favoring rapid larval development.
organicManagement: Harvest at first ripeness in 2-3 day cycles.
Spinosad applications during ripening.
Cold storage immediately.
Cluster bagging on backyard scale.
entries.prevention: Harvest timing. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Pale-yellow oily spots on upper leaf surfaces with white downy sporulation on leaf undersides in humid mornings. Severe infections defoliate vines and cause fruit cluster rot. Can wipe out crops in cool wet seasons.
organicManagement: Copper sprays at first sign of infection and at 7-14 day intervals through humid weather.
Open canopy training and shoot thinning to reduce humidity.
Plant resistant cultivars (Marquette, Frontenac, Petite Pearl, Itasca).
Avoid overhead irrigation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection where possible. Site for air drainage. Open canopy training.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

entries.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves and developing clusters; severe infections cause fruit skin cracking, off-flavors, and reduced yields. Different cultivars vary widely in susceptibility.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at 7-14 day intervals from bud break through veraison.
Open canopy training and shoot thinning.
Plant resistant cultivars (most native American hybrids carry resistance).
Potassium bicarbonate burn-down on visible infections.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Open canopy. Strict spray schedule for susceptible vinifera cultivars.

entries.symptoms: Reddish-brown elongated pustules on stems, leaf sheaths, and leaves; severe infections cause stem lodging and yield loss of 50 percent or more. Ug99 race threatens cultivars worldwide. Cereal rust survives on volunteer wheat and barberry alternate hosts.
organicManagement: Plant rust-resistant cultivars; check USDA-ARS rust survey for current resistant variety list.
Remove volunteer wheat and barberry shrubs (alternate host) from field margins.
Avoid late nitrogen applications that encourage susceptible succulent growth.
Rotate to non-cereal crops for one to two years between wheat crops.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection is primary; resistance breaks every few years and new cultivars must replace older lines.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Cereal Rust Laboratory; Kansas State Extension

entries.symptoms: Small orange-red pustules scattered on leaf surfaces; severe infections reduce photosynthetic area and cause yield loss of 10-30 percent. Most common cereal rust in US wheat-growing regions.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars; rotation of resistance genes through cultivar choice.
Remove volunteer wheat from field margins between crops.
Time planting to avoid the wettest fall infection windows.
Sulfur sprays during early flag-leaf stage on small acreage where economical.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Variety rotation through resistance gene set.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Cereal Rust Laboratory; Kansas State Extension

entries.symptoms: Yellow-orange pustules arranged in stripes parallel to leaf veins; severe infections cause significant yield loss in cool wet springs. Most damaging in the PNW and northern Great Plains.
organicManagement: Plant stripe-rust-resistant cultivars; resistance is the only organic-feasible control at field scale.
Avoid early-planted winter wheat in heavy rust regions.
Remove volunteer wheat in fall before new crop emergence.
Rotate cultivar resistance gene sets across seasons.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Late planting where regionally possible.

entries.symptoms: Bleached premature spikelets in otherwise green heads; pink-orange spore masses on glumes in humid conditions. Severe infections produce shriveled tombstone kernels with mycotoxin contamination (DON, vomitoxin) that makes grain unsalable.
organicManagement: Plant moderately resistant cultivars; pure resistance is not yet available in commercial wheat.
Avoid corn-wheat rotations; corn residue is a major fusarium reservoir.
Bury crop residue with tillage between cereal crops in high-pressure regions.
Time planting to avoid wet weather at flowering.
entries.prevention: Crop rotation away from corn residue. Cultivar selection. Time flowering away from wettest weather windows.
entries.source: USDA-ARS scab initiative; Kansas State Extension

entries.symptoms: Larvae feed at the crown causing stunted plants, lodging, and yield loss. Infested plants are darker green and shorter than healthy plants. Pupae form characteristic flaxseed-shaped puparia near the crown.
organicManagement: Plant after the regional fly-free date (late September to October depending on latitude).
Plant Hessian-fly-resistant cultivars where available.
Plow under or remove volunteer wheat in fall before adult flight.
Avoid continuous wheat; rotate to non-cereal crops.
entries.prevention: Fly-free date planting is the highest-leverage practice. Resistance gene selection through cultivar choice.
entries.source: Kansas State Extension; Penn State Extension

entries.symptoms: Aphids cluster on stems and leaves causing yellowing and stunting; severe infestations transmit Barley Yellow Dwarf Virus (BYDV) which causes leaf reddening and stunting. Adult aphids are winged at peak migration.
organicManagement: Plant later (after peak fall migration) to escape BYDV vectoring.
Encourage parasitic wasp populations (Aphidius) through diverse field-edge habitat.
Insecticidal soap on hot spots of heavy aphid pressure.
Time planting and harvest to avoid peak vector populations.
entries.prevention: Diverse field-edge habitat for natural enemies. Late planting in regions with strong fall BYDV pressure.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as winter wheat stem rust. Spring wheat is exposed to summer rust spores moving northward from southern winter wheat zones; cultivar resistance is mandatory.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivar selection.
Remove volunteer wheat from field margins.
Sound crop rotation.
Sulfur sprays on small acreage.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Regional surveillance through extension reports.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as winter wheat scab. Spring wheat flowering coincides with summer humidity peaks; pressure is heavy in eastern North Dakota and Minnesota production zones.
organicManagement: Cultivar resistance.
Crop rotation away from corn.
Residue burial.
Variety blends to dilute pressure.
entries.prevention: Crop rotation. Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Bright orange pustules on leaves and sheaths; severe infections cause early defoliation and shriveled grain. Alternate host is buckthorn (Rhamnus cathartica).
organicManagement: Plant crown-rust-resistant cultivars.
Remove buckthorn from field margins.
Plant early to escape peak rust window.
Rotate to non-cereal crops between oat crops.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Buckthorn removal.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as wheat stem rust adapted to oat. Long-cycle survival on barberry; modern epidemiology depends on overwintering on susceptible oats in milder zones.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Remove volunteer oats and barberry.
Crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Small orange pustules scattered on leaves. Barley leaf rust is genetically distinct from wheat leaf rust; cultivar resistance is separate. Heavy infections reduce grain quality for malting.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars.
Remove volunteer barley.
Rotation to non-cereal crops.
Sulfur sprays on small acreage.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Sound rotation.

entries.symptoms: Grain heads filled with dark spore masses (smutted) replacing normal grain. Spores released at threshing contaminate next year's seed and infect new plants at germination.
organicManagement: Plant certified disease-free seed.
Hot-water seed treatment (52C for 10 minutes) effective on small lots.
Crop rotation away from infected fields.
Plant resistant cultivars where available.
entries.prevention: Seed selection. Hot-water treatment for saved seed.

entries.symptoms: Dark purple-black hard sclerotia replace grain kernels in heads; sclerotia contain ergotamine alkaloids highly toxic to humans and livestock. Single contaminated batch can poison entire grain lots.
organicManagement: Plant certified ergot-free seed.
Mow field margins and ditch banks where wild grasses host ergot.
Deep plow infected residue to bury sclerotia.
Sound crop rotation; ergot sclerotia lose viability after 1-2 years of buried storage.
entries.prevention: Seed cleanliness through certified sources. Crop rotation. Margin grass management.
entries.source: USDA-ARS ergot research; North Dakota State Extension

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as wheat stem rust. Rye is generally more rust-tolerant than wheat; severe infections still cause yield loss in susceptible cultivars.
organicManagement: Cultivar selection.
Volunteer rye removal.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Sound rotation.

entries.symptoms: Beetle larvae feed on corn roots causing lodging in adult plants; root pruning reduces water uptake and yield. Adult beetles feed on silks reducing pollination. Western corn rootworm is the dominant species in modern continuous-corn rotations.
organicManagement: Crop rotation: one year of any non-corn crop breaks the cycle for non-resistant beetle populations.
Beneficial nematodes (Heterorhabditis) drenched at planting target larvae.
Trap crops of pumpkin or squash at field margins.
Plant resistant cultivars (Bt-corn rootworm) only where conventional management allows.
entries.prevention: Annual rotation away from corn is the single highest-leverage practice. Continuous corn requires more complex management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on sweet corn: larvae tunnel through stalks causing breakage and into ears causing damage. Three generations per year in southern regions.
organicManagement: Trichogramma releases at first egg lay.
Bt sprays at first whorl through silking.
Bt-Cry traits where available.
Remove and destroy crop residue in fall.
entries.prevention: Field sanitation through residue management. Trap crops of millet at field edges.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Iowa State Extension

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on sweet corn: silver-gray galls on ears and tassels filled with black spores. Heritage flint corn cultivars are particularly susceptible; the gall fungus is also harvested as huitlacoche delicacy.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars where available.
Crop rotation.
Remove infected plants before galls burst and release spores.
Accept on home-scale plantings for huitlacoche harvest.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on flint corn. Dent corn is the dominant US field corn type; rootworm has been the dominant insect problem of continuous-corn rotations.
organicManagement: Annual rotation away from corn.
Beneficial nematodes at planting.
Trap crops.
entries.prevention: Crop rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on flint corn. Heritage open-pollinated dent corn varieties are highly susceptible; modern hybrids carry Bt traits in conventional management.
organicManagement: Trichogramma releases.
Bt sprays.
Sanitation through residue management.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Larvae feed in whorls of young corn; ragged-edge defoliation with frass accumulation in whorl. Late-season generations damage ears. Migratory pest; northern incursions follow southern population buildups.
organicManagement: Bt sprays at first whorl through silking.
Trichogramma releases timed to first egg lay.
Encourage parasitic wasps and ground beetles through diverse plantings.
Time planting to avoid peak generations where possible.
entries.prevention: Monitoring through pheromone traps. Habitat diversity.

entries.symptoms: Tiny orange flies lay eggs in flowering florets; larvae feed inside developing seeds causing seed sterility. Heavy infestations reduce yield by 50 percent or more.
organicManagement: Plant early-maturing cultivars to escape peak midge generations.
Synchronize blooming across the field to limit egg-laying window.
Encourage parasitic wasp populations through diverse plantings.
Plant midge-resistant cultivars where available.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Bloom synchronization through uniform planting.

entries.symptoms: Yellow-tan aphids cluster on leaf undersides; severe infestations cause honeydew accumulation, sooty mold, leaf curl, and stunted plants. Invasive since 2013; rapidly transformed southern US sorghum production.
organicManagement: Plant tolerant or resistant cultivars where available.
Encourage parasitic wasp (Aphelinus) and predator (Coccinella) populations through diverse field-edge habitat.
Insecticidal soap on hot spots.
Time planting to escape peak aphid populations.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Diverse predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Yellow streaks on leaves; severe infections produce sterile spike-like flower heads (green-ear disease). Most damaging in humid late-summer conditions.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars.
Time planting to limit flowering during wettest weather.
Crop rotation away from millet.
Remove and destroy infected plants.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: True bug adults and nymphs feed on flowering amaranth heads causing seed sterility and reduced grain yield. Lygus species; broad host range across many crops.
organicManagement: Spinosad sprays during peak feeding.
White sticky traps for monitoring.
Encourage minute pirate bug populations through diverse plantings.
Time harvest before peak nymph populations build.
entries.prevention: Field-edge predator habitat. Trap monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Pale yellow leaf lesions with gray downy sporulation on undersides; severe infections cause defoliation and reduced seed fill. Cool wet conditions favor infection.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars from Andean breeding programs.
Site for air drainage to reduce humidity.
Crop rotation away from quinoa.
Remove crop residue to reduce overwintering spore reservoirs.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Rotation.
entries.source: Washington State University quinoa research; CIP-International Potato Center

entries.symptoms: Pale yellow patches on upper leaf surfaces with gray-purple downy sporulation on undersides. Spreads explosively in humid conditions; can destroy a basil crop in 7-10 days. Genovese basil is highly susceptible.
organicManagement: Plant downy-mildew-resistant cultivars (Prospera, Eleonora, Amazel, Devotion).
Site basil in well-ventilated locations with morning sun.
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the base only.
Remove infected plants immediately at first symptoms.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Indoor production with controlled humidity. Inspect new plants for symptoms before transplanting.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension basil disease research; USDA-ARS

entries.symptoms: Adult beetles skeletonize basil leaves leaving lacy patterns. Basil is a favored host; rapid feeding can defoliate small plantings.
organicManagement: Hand-pick adults into soapy water in early morning.
Surround (kaolin clay) sprays as feeding deterrent.
Milky spore application on adjacent lawn for long-term grub suppression.
Cover plants with floating row cover during peak adult flight.
entries.prevention: Lawn grub management. Avoid pheromone traps; they attract more beetles than they catch.

entries.symptoms: Orange spore pustules on leaf undersides in spring and early summer; severe infections cause leaf drop and weakened plants. Mint rust is genetically distinct from other rusts; affects only the Mentha genus.
organicManagement: Cut and destroy infected stems immediately.
Apply propane flaming to infected ground in winter (kills overwintering spores).
Mulch heavily to suppress soil spore reservoirs.
Plant resistant cultivars where available.
entries.prevention: Sanitation of infected stems. Mulch management. Avoid sharing plant divisions from infected plants.

entries.symptoms: Tiny mites on leaf undersides; fine stippling on upper leaf surfaces, severe infestations cause webbing and leaf bronzing. Hot dry conditions favor outbreaks.
organicManagement: Strong water spray dislodges mites; repeat every 3-4 days.
Insecticidal soap on hot spots.
Release predatory mites (Phytoseiulus) on indoor or greenhouse mint.
Maintain consistent soil moisture; drought stress amplifies mite damage.
entries.prevention: Avoid water-stressing plants. Diverse predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on spearmint. Peppermint is equally susceptible; commercial peppermint production manages rust as the most significant disease.
organicManagement: Sanitation pruning.
Propane flaming in winter.
Crop rotation in field production.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Wilting and yellowing followed by sudden plant death; roots show brown soft decay. Phytophthora and Rhizoctonia species cause root rot in poorly drained soils.
organicManagement: Plant in well-drained soil; raised beds for heavy clay sites.
Avoid overhead irrigation; water deeply and infrequently at the base.
Mulch with gravel or sand for crown air circulation.
Remove and destroy infected plants; do not replant lavender in the same spot for 3+ years.
entries.prevention: Site selection: well-drained soil. Raised bed culture. Avoid overhead watering.
entries.source: OSU Extension; UC ANR Master Gardener

entries.symptoms: Bright yellow mosaic pattern on leaves; reduced flower production and weak growth. Aphid-vectored from alfalfa and clover hosts.
organicManagement: Remove and destroy infected plants.
Manage aphid populations on nearby legume hosts.
Use insect netting on commercial-scale lavender nurseries.
Avoid sharing plant divisions from symptomatic stock.
entries.prevention: Source from disease-free nurseries. Aphid management.
entries.source: UC ANR Master Gardener; OSU Extension

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on mint: leaf stippling and webbing on stressed plants. Indoor oregano in winter is most vulnerable.
organicManagement: Water spray.
Insecticidal soap.
Maintain consistent moisture.
entries.prevention: Avoid drought stress.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic: leaf stippling and webbing. Thyme grown indoors or in greenhouses in winter is vulnerable; outdoor field thyme rarely sees heavy mite pressure.
organicManagement: Water spray.
Insecticidal soap on heavy infestations.
entries.prevention: Avoid water stress.

entries.symptoms: White powdery growth on leaves and stems; severe infections cause leaf yellowing and reduced vigor. Most common in humid summers.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at 7-10 day intervals during active growth.
Open the plant with selective pruning for airflow.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
Plant in well-ventilated sites.
entries.prevention: Site for air drainage. Pruning for airflow.

entries.symptoms: Wilting and yellowing followed by plant death; brown soft roots. Phytophthora root rot is the primary cause; particularly damaging in poorly drained or overwatered plantings.
organicManagement: Plant in well-drained soil.
Avoid overwatering.
Raised beds in heavy clay sites.
Replace infected plants in a different location.
entries.prevention: Site selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard blueberry SWD. Bluecrop's heavy commercial cropping makes precise management critical.
organicManagement: Exclusion netting.
Spinosad sprays during ripening.
Cold storage immediately.
entries.prevention: Exclusion netting.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard blueberry mummy berry. Northern highbush types are highly susceptible; sanitation pruning and mulching are essential.
organicManagement: Fall cultivation under bushes.
Heavy mulch.
Copper sprays.
Cultivar selection.
entries.prevention: Sanitation through cultivation. Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard blueberry SWD. Rabbiteye types ripen later than northern highbush; harvest extends into peak SWD population windows.
organicManagement: Exclusion netting.
Spinosad sprays.
Cold storage.
entries.prevention: Exclusion netting.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard blueberry maggot. Wild and managed lowbush blueberry plantings in northern New England are the species' historic range.
organicManagement: Sticky trap monitoring.
Spinosad bait sprays.
Drop berry sanitation.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard raspberry SWD. Heritage is a fall-bearing primocane cultivar; September-October harvest overlaps peak SWD populations.
organicManagement: Exclusion netting on trellis rows.
Spinosad sprays during ripening.
Cold storage.
entries.prevention: Netting.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard raspberry. Canby is a thornless red raspberry; the open canopy actually reduces anthracnose pressure compared to dense thorny cultivars.
organicManagement: Lime sulfur dormant spray.
Open row pruning.
Annual cane sanitation.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard blackberry SWD. Triple Crown is a thornless mid-season cultivar; harvest July-August overlaps peak SWD populations.
organicManagement: Exclusion netting.
Spinosad sprays.
entries.prevention: Netting.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard strawberry SWD. Albion is a day-neutral cultivar fruiting from spring through fall; extended harvest exposes fruit to multiple SWD generations.
organicManagement: Exclusion netting through summer.
Spinosad sprays.
Cold storage immediately after harvest.
Tight 2-day harvest cycle.
entries.prevention: Netting.

entries.symptoms: Fuzzy gray spore masses on ripening fruit, especially fruit touching wet soil. Spreads rapidly in humid pre-harvest weather; can destroy a crop within 48 hours.
organicManagement: Straw mulch under fruit.
Drip irrigation only; no overhead.
Harvest dry fruit; remove and destroy infected berries.
Open row spacing for airflow.
entries.prevention: Mulch. Drip irrigation. Open spacing.

entries.symptoms: Lygus bug nymphs and adults feed on flowering strawberries causing distorted catfaced fruit. Everbearing cultivars with continuous flowering are highly exposed to season-long bug pressure.
organicManagement: White sticky traps for monitoring.
Spinosad on hot spots during peak nymph populations.
Encourage minute pirate bug habitat through diverse field-edge plantings.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring. Diverse predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard strawberry. Alpine strawberries are small and continuous-fruiting; gray mold spreads less aggressively due to the open habit but still affects ripening fruit.
organicManagement: Mulch.
Drip irrigation.
Remove infected berries.
entries.prevention: Mulch and drip.

entries.symptoms: Microscopic mites (only visible with 10x hand lens) on stems and lower leaves; affected stems and leaves develop bronze russet color, lower leaves wilt and drop. Plant declines from the bottom up.
organicManagement: Sulfur dust applied to lower stems and soil surface.
Insecticidal soap on hot spots.
Remove and destroy heavily infested lower leaves.
Sanitation: remove all crop debris in fall.
entries.prevention: Scout regularly with hand lens. Sanitation between seasons.

entries.symptoms: Yellowing and upward leaf curl with leaf cupping; severe stunting and reduced flowering. Whitefly-vectored; spreads rapidly through whitefly populations.
organicManagement: Plant TYLCV-resistant cultivars where available.
Aggressive whitefly management with yellow sticky traps and insecticidal soap.
Use insect netting on plants during high-pressure periods.
Remove infected plants immediately.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Whitefly management. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Larvae bore into peppers through the calyx; entry holes are obscured but internal feeding causes fruit rot. Sweet peppers near corn fields show heaviest pressure.
organicManagement: Bt sprays at flowering through fruit set.
Trichogramma releases.
Pheromone trap monitoring for flight timing.
Distance from corn fields where possible.
entries.prevention: Spatial separation from corn. Monitoring.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Iowa State Extension

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on sweet pepper. Hot pepper cultivars vary widely in susceptibility; jalapeno types are particularly susceptible.
organicManagement: Raised beds.
Cultivar selection.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Drainage. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Leaf stippling and webbing on hot pepper plants, particularly in hot dry conditions. Some hot pepper cultivars actually tolerate mite damage better than sweet types.
organicManagement: Water spray.
Insecticidal soap.
Predatory mites in greenhouse production.
entries.prevention: Avoid water stress.

entries.symptoms: Rough corky raised lesions on tuber surfaces; cosmetic damage that does not affect storage life but affects market quality. Caused by Streptomyces scabies; favored by dry alkaline soils.
organicManagement: Maintain soil pH below 5.5 through sulfur amendment.
Irrigate consistently during tuber bulking to prevent dry stress.
Plant scab-resistant cultivars (Russet Burbank is susceptible; many newer varieties are resistant).
Crop rotation (3 years away from potato).
entries.prevention: Soil pH management. Consistent irrigation. Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Larvae tunnel through leaves, stems, and tubers; entry holes in tubers leave tunnels filled with frass. Storage tuberworm causes major losses in stored potatoes.
organicManagement: Hill soil deeply over developing tubers to prevent surface laying.
Harvest promptly when foliage dies; do not leave tubers in soil exposed.
Cold storage immediately after harvest (40F) suppresses larvae.
Bt or spinosad foliar sprays during heavy adult flight.
entries.prevention: Hill management. Prompt harvest. Cool storage.

entries.symptoms: Yellow witches'-broom of carrot tops; hairy distorted roots with bitter flavor. Leafhopper-vectored phytoplasma; affects many crops including carrot, lettuce, celery.
organicManagement: Manage leafhopper populations with kaolin clay and reflective mulches.
Remove and destroy infected plants.
Plant resistant cultivars where available.
Avoid planting near infected lettuce or other host crops.
entries.prevention: Leafhopper management. Sanitation.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; University of Wisconsin Extension

entries.symptoms: Yellow ring patterns on leaves; severe infections cause leaf collapse and reduced bulb size. Thrips-vectored from infected iris, onion, and weed hosts.
organicManagement: Manage thrips populations with blue sticky traps and reflective mulches.
Remove cull onion piles and weed hosts.
Plant tolerant cultivars where available.
Avoid planting near infected weed populations.
entries.prevention: Thrips management. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Stunted plants with yellowing leaves; bulbs are smaller, soft, with brown discoloration. Stem and bulb nematode (Ditylenchus dipsaci) is the primary garlic pest worldwide.
organicManagement: Plant only certified nematode-free seed garlic.
Hot-water seed treatment (44C for 20 minutes) sanitizes saved seed.
Crop rotation (4+ years away from alliums).
Soil solarization in heavily infested beds.
entries.prevention: Certified seed. Hot-water treatment. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Purple-brown elliptical lesions on leaves with concentric ring patterns; severe infections cause leaf dieback and reduced bulb yield.
organicManagement: Crop rotation (3+ years away from alliums).
Open spacing for airflow.
Sulfur sprays at first symptoms.
Remove crop residue in fall.
entries.prevention: Rotation. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Adult fly larvae mine through stems creating brown winding tunnels; weakens stems and reduces vigor. Spread of asparagus fusarium pathogen complex linked to miner activity.
organicManagement: Maintain plant vigor through proper fertility and irrigation.
Remove and destroy infested stems.
Encourage predatory wasp populations through diverse plantings.
Manage Fusarium with all available cultural practices.
entries.prevention: Vigor management. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Small moth larvae mine through leek leaves leaving silvery trails; severe infestations bore into leaf bases causing rot. Two generations per year.
organicManagement: Row covers from planting through first generation.
Bt sprays during young larval stage.
Pheromone trap monitoring for flight timing.
Crop rotation away from alliums.
entries.prevention: Row cover. Trap monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on onion thrips: silvery streaks on leaves from sap-feeding. Severe infestations stunt leek growth and reduce stem quality.
organicManagement: Blue sticky traps for monitoring.
Insecticidal soap on hot spots.
Reflective mulches deter thrips.
entries.prevention: Reflective mulch. Trap monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as garlic bloat nematode. Shallot is in the same allium species as onion; nematode pressure is identical.
organicManagement: Certified nematode-free seed.
Hot-water treatment.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Certified seed. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Brown leaf margin scorch starting on one side of the plant; wilting that does not recover overnight; brown discoloration in stem vascular bundles when cut. Seedborne; spreads through pruning wounds and splash dispersal.
organicManagement: Plant certified disease-free seed.
Hot-water seed treatment (50C for 25 minutes) sanitizes saved seed.
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately.
Avoid wounding plants during management; sterilize tools.
entries.prevention: Seed source. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cabbage. Kale's curled leaves harbor aphids in crevices that are hard to spray; insecticidal soap penetration is challenging.
organicManagement: Strong water spray to dislodge aphids.
Insecticidal soap applications focused under leaves.
Encourage ladybug and lacewing populations.
Remove heavily infested lower leaves.
entries.prevention: Diverse predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Small caterpillars create window-pane feeding damage on leaves; older larvae bore into leaf folds. Migratory; develops insecticide resistance rapidly in continuous-cropping zones.
organicManagement: Bt sprays.
Spinosad in rotation with Bt to slow resistance.
Row cover.
Trichogramma releases at first egg lay.
entries.prevention: Pheromone trap monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Adult fly larvae mine winding trails between leaf surfaces; serpentine pale tunnels visible on spinach leaves. Severe infestations reduce leaf quality and yield.
organicManagement: Row cover from emergence until flight ends.
Remove and destroy mined leaves before larvae mature.
Encourage parasitoid wasp populations.
Crop rotation away from spinach beds.
entries.prevention: Row cover. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on broader lettuce-aphid coverage. Lettuce aphid hides inside head and is particularly difficult to control during harvest; major contaminant concern in commercial production.
organicManagement: Insecticidal soap sprays focused on developing heads.
Encourage ladybug and lacewing populations.
Plant aphid-resistant cultivars.
Reflective mulches deter winged aphid arrival.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Reflective mulch.

entries.symptoms: Tan circular spots on celery leaves and petioles with concentric ring patterns; severe infections cause leaf collapse. Cercospora apii (early blight of celery, distinct from tomato early blight).
organicManagement: Crop rotation (3+ years).
Open row spacing for airflow.
Copper sprays at first symptoms.
Plant resistant cultivars where available.
entries.prevention: Rotation. Spacing.

entries.symptoms: Yellow patches on leaves becoming brown with concentric rings; severe infections cause complete defoliation. Septoria apiicola; cool wet conditions favor infection.
organicManagement: Crop rotation.
Open row spacing.
Copper sprays.
Sanitation of crop residue.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cucurbit fusarium: wilting and yellowing with brown vascular discoloration in stems. Soilborne; favored by warm humid conditions.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Crop rotation (4+ years).
Raised beds for drainage.
Solarization.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as cucurbit fusarium. Watermelon is highly susceptible; race-specific resistance is in many commercial cultivars.
organicManagement: Plant race-specific resistant cultivars.
Crop rotation (5+ years out of cucurbits).
Solarization.
Grafting onto resistant rootstocks (commercial scale).
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Rotation. Grafting.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on other cucurbits: white powdery growth on leaves. Cantaloupe is highly susceptible; powdery mildew shortens harvest window significantly.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Resistant cultivars.
Open spacing.
Potassium bicarbonate burn-down.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as watermelon fusarium wilt. Honeydew melon shares the cucurbit Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. melonis pathogen.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Rotation.
Grafting.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as cantaloupe powdery mildew. Honeydew is similarly susceptible.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Resistant cultivars.
Open spacing.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as other cucurbit powdery mildew. Pumpkin's heavy foliage and dense canopy amplify mildew pressure; standard control on commercial pumpkin operations.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at 7-day intervals.
Resistant cultivars (PMR varieties).
Open spacing.
Potassium bicarbonate.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Small white spindle-shaped lesions on stems and petioles; severe infections cause stem dieback and reduced yield. Mostly an East Coast pumpkin pathogen.
organicManagement: Crop rotation (3+ years).
Resistant cultivars where available.
Open row spacing.
Copper sprays.
entries.prevention: Rotation. Cultivar selection.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

entries.symptoms: Sudden yellowing of vines followed by death within 7-10 days; phloem-limited bacterium vectored by squash bug. Most damaging in southern Plains and southern US.
organicManagement: Manage squash bug populations aggressively.
Row covers until first flower.
Resistant cultivars where available.
Remove infected plants immediately.
entries.prevention: Squash bug management.

entries.symptoms: Green powdery mold on log surfaces and on cut ends; the green color is mature spore mass. Aggressive competitor that can colonize logs before or after shiitake inoculation, replacing the cultivated mycelium.
organicManagement: Inoculate logs with high-quality, vigorous shiitake spawn within 2-4 weeks of cutting.
Wax cut ends and spawn holes promptly to prevent green-mold colonization.
Maintain proper log moisture (35-45 percent); excessive moisture favors trichoderma.
Discard severely infected logs immediately; do not stack with healthy logs.
entries.prevention: Cut logs in winter or early spring before sap rises and before trichoderma spore peaks. Use only certified-pure spawn from reliable suppliers.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program mushroom cultivation; Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.symptoms: Large bracket fungus fruiting bodies on log sides; brown to gray colored shelf-like structures. Aggressive primary decomposer that displaces shiitake mycelium and rapidly destroys log structural integrity.
organicManagement: Inspect new logs for existing ganoderma colonization before inoculation.
Discard infected logs (do not compost where shiitake stacks are stored).
Maintain log stacks away from fallen forest hardwoods carrying ganoderma.
Refresh substrate every 3-4 years before ganoderma typically arrives.
entries.prevention: Substrate freshness through annual log refresh.

entries.symptoms: Holes chewed through young pin-stage mushrooms and through mature shiitake caps. Slugs concentrate at log stacks in wet weather; severe damage in maritime climates.
organicManagement: Iron phosphate (Sluggo, OMRI) sprinkled around log stacks during fruiting flushes.
Beer traps placed near log stacks attract slugs to drown.
Hand-pick slugs at dusk during fruiting flushes.
Diatomaceous earth barrier around log stacks.
entries.prevention: Maintain log stacks elevated off ground on rails. Reduce surrounding habitat for slugs.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; OSU Extension

entries.symptoms: Tiny black gnats around log stacks and indoor fruiting blocks; larvae feed in moist substrate and on mushroom pin tissue. Disrupts pinning and causes cosmetic damage.
organicManagement: Yellow sticky traps near log stacks.
Bacillus thuringiensis var. israelensis (Bti) drenched on substrate.
Beneficial nematodes (Steinernema feltiae) applied to substrate.
Reduce excess moisture in indoor fruiting rooms.
entries.prevention: Moisture management. Sticky traps for monitoring.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.symptoms: Fast-growing white-gray fluffy mold across substrate surface; covers mushroom pins and pinheads, preventing fruiting. Distinguished from oyster mycelium by faster spread and webby texture.
organicManagement: Reduce humidity below 80 percent during pin-set window.
Discard infected blocks; do not attempt remediation.
Improve sanitation of cultivation rooms; cobweb spores are airborne and persistent.
Spray salt water (3 percent salt solution) on early infections to inhibit spread.
entries.prevention: Strict sanitation protocols. Humidity management during pinning.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running; Cornell Small Farms Program

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as for shiitake. Oyster substrate (straw or wood chip) is highly attractive to fungus gnats; indoor production rooms must control humidity tightly.
organicManagement: Yellow sticky traps.
Bti drench.
Beneficial nematodes.
Humidity management.
entries.prevention: Sticky trap monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Yellow or brown sticky lesions on cap surfaces; pin tissue softens and degrades. Pseudomonas tolaasii is the primary pathogen.
organicManagement: Reduce surface moisture on caps; ventilate fruiting rooms.
Salt-water spray treatment on early infections.
Discard severely infected blocks.
Sterile substrate preparation.
entries.prevention: Humidity management. Substrate sterilization.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on pearl oyster cobweb. King oyster's slow pin development gives cobweb more time to colonize substrate; sanitation is critical.
organicManagement: Strict sanitation.
Humidity below 85 percent.
Salt-water spray on early infections.
Discard infected blocks.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic: green powdery mold on substrate surface. Lion's mane is slow-growing and particularly vulnerable to trichoderma overgrowth during the long colonization phase.
organicManagement: Sterile substrate preparation (autoclave or pressure cooker).
Use only certified-pure lion's mane spawn.
Maintain substrate at proper moisture (60-65 percent).
Discard infected blocks promptly.
entries.prevention: Sterile substrate. Pure spawn.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Wine cap is robust and competitive in outdoor wood-chip beds; trichoderma is rarely a serious problem in well-established outdoor patches but can damage indoor sterile-substrate production.
organicManagement: Use vigorous spawn at full strength (5-10 percent w/w on substrate).
Maintain wood-chip beds in dappled shade with consistent moisture.
Refresh mulch annually with new wood chips.
Avoid using composted wood chips that may carry trichoderma.
entries.prevention: Fresh wood-chip substrate. Established mycelium outcompetes trichoderma in outdoor patches.
entries.source: Stamets, Mycelium Running; Cornell Small Farms Program

entries.symptoms: Slugs chew through young wine cap pins and through mature caps. Wine cap fruits prolifically in moist outdoor beds where slug populations are also high.
organicManagement: Iron phosphate (Sluggo) sprinkled around fruiting flushes.
Hand-pick slugs at dusk.
Beer traps near fruiting patches.
Maintain dry pathways adjacent to wine cap beds.
entries.prevention: Habitat management for slug populations.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on oyster. Almond agaricus (Agaricus subrufescens) is grown on composted substrate; bacterial blotch develops in high-humidity fruiting rooms.
organicManagement: Humidity management.
Salt-water spray treatment.
Sterile substrate.
entries.prevention: Substrate management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic: green powdery mold contamination. Morel cultivation requires exceptionally clean substrate due to the slow morel growth rate.
organicManagement: Sterile substrate preparation.
Use only verified morel spawn from research-scale suppliers.
Climate-controlled cultivation rooms.
Discard infected substrate immediately.
entries.prevention: Sterile technique. Modern morel cultivation is research-scale; small-scale success rates are very low.

entries.symptoms: Wild ganoderma applanatum colonizing reishi (Ganoderma lucidum) cultivation logs can produce mixed fruiting and confuse identification. The competition can also reduce reishi yield.
organicManagement: Source logs from healthy hardwood; inspect for existing ganoderma.
Use verified reishi spawn at high inoculation rates.
Sterilize logs (autoclave or steam) before inoculation.
Maintain logs in dappled shade with controlled moisture.
entries.prevention: Substrate cleanliness. Spawn quality.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Maitake (Grifola frondosa) cultivation on hardwood blocks is highly sensitive to trichoderma contamination; sterile substrate is essential.
organicManagement: Sterile sawdust substrate preparation.
Vigorous maitake spawn at high inoculation rates.
Climate-controlled fruiting rooms.
Discard contaminated blocks.
entries.prevention: Sterile technique.
entries.source: Cornell Small Farms Program; Stamets, Mycelium Running

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Turkey tail (Trametes versicolor) is competitive and outcompetes trichoderma in established cultivation; early-stage contamination is the primary risk.
organicManagement: Sterile substrate.
Vigorous spawn at high inoculation rates.
Maintain proper substrate moisture.
entries.prevention: Sterile technique.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Chicken of the woods (Laetiporus) is typically cultivated outdoors on stumps; trichoderma contamination is less common in field settings than in indoor sterile-block production.
organicManagement: Use fresh hardwood stumps and logs.
Verified spawn at high inoculation rates.
Outdoor placement in dappled shade.
entries.prevention: Substrate freshness.

entries.symptoms: Larvae feed on alfalfa leaves and stems creating ragged defoliation; severe infestations cause silver-gray skeletonized appearance across whole fields. Adult weevils oviposit in stems during fall and early spring.
organicManagement: Time first cutting to remove larvae with the cut hay.
Encourage parasitic wasp (Bathyplectes) populations through diverse field-edge plantings.
Beneficial nematodes (Steinernema) for spot treatment of heavy infestations.
Crop rotation in heavily infested fields.
entries.prevention: Early first cutting. Predator habitat.
entries.source: USDA-ARS alfalfa research; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as potato leafhopper on potato: yellow V-shaped tip burn on leaves (hopperburn). Severe infestations reduce alfalfa yield and protein content.
organicManagement: Time cuttings to remove adults with the cut hay.
Manage field-edge weed populations that harbor leafhoppers.
Plant glandular-haired alfalfa cultivars resistant to leafhopper feeding.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Cutting timing.

entries.symptoms: Yellow chevrons on leaves followed by entire plant wilt; severe infections kill stands. Verticillium alboatrum; spreads in equipment between fields.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars; modern breeding programs prioritize Verticillium resistance.
Sound crop rotation (4+ years between alfalfa crops).
Sanitize equipment between fields.
Avoid pasturing or grazing infected fields.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Brown stem lesions with darker margins; severe infections cause stem dieback and reduced stand vigor. Most damaging in warm humid conditions.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Crop rotation.
Avoid late-season planting that exposes young stands to disease pressure.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Beetle larvae feed in roots and crowns of red clover plants; severe infestations cause stand thinning and reduced productivity. Two-year stand cycles are typical in heavily infested regions.
organicManagement: Plow under stands every two years to interrupt the life cycle.
Crop rotation away from clover for 3+ years.
Plant clover after a non-host crop year.
entries.prevention: Short stand rotation.

entries.symptoms: Dark stem lesions causing stem dieback; severe infections reduce stand productivity. Most damaging in humid northern regions.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Sound crop rotation.
Avoid mowing during wet conditions when spores spread.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on red clover. White clover is less affected than red clover due to its stoloniferous habit; pressure is generally low.
organicManagement: Maintain healthy stand vigor.
Diverse predator habitat.
entries.prevention: Vigor management.

entries.symptoms: Larvae feed on clover leaves creating ragged defoliation; can reduce biomass in heavily infested stands. Annual clover habit limits long-term pressure.
organicManagement: Time mow-down to limit larval development.
Encourage parasitic wasp populations.
Sound crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Mow timing.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as pea leaf weevil on peas: notched leaf margins from adult feeding, plus root nodule damage from larvae. Hairy vetch is a host species.
organicManagement: Time planting to limit weevil establishment.
Encourage parasitoid populations.
Crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Beetle larvae feed inside developing seeds; emerged adults leave round exit holes in stored seed. Important when saving seed from vetch crops; less significant in cover-crop systems where vetch is killed before seed maturity.
organicManagement: Kill vetch before seed maturity for cover-crop use.
For seed production, harvest at first seed maturity and store cold (below 50F).
Sound crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Early mow-down for cover crop use.

entries.symptoms: Adult beetles feed on buckwheat foliage and flowers; severe infestations reduce seed set. Buckwheat's fast growth often outpaces moderate beetle pressure.
organicManagement: Accept moderate damage given fast crop cycle.
Time planting to avoid peak beetle flight where possible.
Surround (kaolin clay) sprays for heavy infestations.
entries.prevention: Timing.

entries.symptoms: Pale yellow patches on leaves with downy gray sporulation on undersides. Buckwheat's fast growth and quick maturity limit disease impact.
organicManagement: Accept moderate damage.
Crop rotation.
Open spacing.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on wheat. Winter rye is the most aphid-tolerant cereal; pressure is generally low even with moderate aphid populations.
organicManagement: Accept moderate populations.
Diverse predator habitat.
Time cover-crop kill to manage virus reservoir.
entries.prevention: Habitat management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on rye grain. Cover-crop rye killed before seed maturity bypasses ergot concerns; grain-harvest rye must manage as for rye-grain.
organicManagement: Kill cover crop before seed maturity.
If saving seed, sound sanitation and margin grass management.
entries.prevention: Kill timing.

entries.symptoms: Yellow-orange leaf discoloration starting from leaf tips; stunted plants with reduced yield. Aphid-vectored from infected wild grasses and volunteer cereals.
organicManagement: Plant later to escape peak fall aphid migration.
Manage aphid populations with insecticidal soap on hot spots.
Plant tolerant cultivars where available.
Remove volunteer cereals from field margins.
entries.prevention: Timing. Aphid management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on garden peas. Field pea cover-crop stands tolerate moderate aphid populations; cover-crop function is unaffected.
organicManagement: Accept moderate populations.
Encourage predators.
Cover-crop kill before peak aphid populations.
entries.prevention: Crop timing.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Austrian winter pea cover-crop stands tolerate moderate aphid pressure.
organicManagement: Accept moderate populations.
Predator habitat.
entries.prevention: Habitat.

entries.symptoms: Microscopic mites cluster in timothy leaf sheaths causing yellowing and weakening. Severe infestations reduce hay yield and quality.
organicManagement: Time first cutting to remove mite populations with the cut hay.
Sulfur dust on hot spots in seed-production fields.
Crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Cutting timing.

entries.symptoms: Endophyte-infected tall fescue produces ergovaline toxin causing rough hair coat, reduced weight gain, and fescue foot in grazing livestock. Affected forage looks normal; toxicity is internal.
organicManagement: Plant novel-endophyte (low-ergovaline) cultivars.
Replace toxic-endophyte stands with novel-endophyte or alternative grasses.
Dilute fescue with companion legumes to reduce toxicosis exposure.
Pasture rotation during ergovaline peak periods (summer).
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Yellow stripe rust pustules on leaves; reduces forage quality and seed yield. Cool wet conditions favor infection.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Avoid over-fertilization that produces susceptible growth.
Crop rotation in seed-production fields.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on corn. Sorghum-sudangrass is a major fall armyworm host in southern US production zones.
organicManagement: Time cuttings to remove larvae with the cut hay.
Bt sprays during young larval stage.
Trichogramma releases.
Pheromone trap monitoring.
entries.prevention: Cutting timing. Monitoring.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension; UF/IFAS Extension

entries.symptoms: Drought-stressed or frost-damaged sorghum-sudan accumulates cyanogenic glycosides that release hydrogen cyanide. Affected forage looks normal; toxicity is acute in livestock.
organicManagement: Do not graze drought-stressed or frost-damaged sorghum-sudan.
Wait 7-10 days after frost before grazing or cutting for hay.
Wait until plants reach 24 inches before grazing or harvesting.
Test forage where uncertainty exists about toxicity levels.
entries.prevention: Management timing. Plant maturity before grazing.

entries.symptoms: Brown stem lesions causing dieback; severe infections reduce stand productivity. Most damaging in cool wet conditions.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Crop rotation.
Avoid mowing during wet weather.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: White powdery growth on leaves; severe infections reduce forage quality and palatability.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Avoid over-fertilization.
Crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Tiny mites with two distinctive dark spots on the body; fine stippling and bronzing on leaves; webbing on undersides during heavy infestations. Hot dry conditions amplify outbreaks.
organicManagement: Water spray dislodges mites; repeat every 3 days.
Insecticidal soap focused on leaf undersides.
Predatory mites (Phytoseiulus) for greenhouse production.
Maintain consistent soil moisture; drought stress amplifies damage.
entries.prevention: Avoid water stress. Diverse predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Caterpillars cut transplants at the soil line; rapid wilt and death of young plants. Active at night; hide in soil during the day.
organicManagement: Cardboard or aluminum foil collars around transplant stems.
Bt sprays during active larval feeding.
Hand-pick at dusk from disturbed soil.
Fall tilling exposes pupae to predators and freezing.
entries.prevention: Collar protection. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Adult weevils feed on flower buds and lay eggs in developing fruit; larvae feed inside fruit causing premature drop. Heavy infestations destroy entire pepper crops.
organicManagement: Pheromone trap monitoring for first flight detection.
Spinosad sprays at first flight.
Remove and destroy fallen fruit weekly.
Crop rotation away from peppers and tomato family.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Angular leaf lesions delimited by veins; lesions become tan with yellow halos. Cool wet conditions favor infection. Pseudomonas syringae pv. lachrymans.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars.
Crop rotation (3+ years).
Copper sprays at first symptoms.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on zucchini. Summer squash hybrids vary in susceptibility; butternut and cushaw types are most resistant.
organicManagement: Stem wrap.
Bt injection.
Hand-knife removal.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on winter squash: gray-brown bugs in clusters causing leaf yellowing and wilt; egg masses on leaf undersides.
organicManagement: Hand-pick adults and egg masses daily.
Trap boards under plants for daytime adult resting.
Spinosad on heavy infestations of nymphs.
Row cover until first flower.
entries.prevention: Daily monitoring during peak nymph emergence.

entries.symptoms: Dark brown leaf lesions with yellow halos; severe infections cause leaf collapse and reduced root size. Most damaging in warm humid conditions.
organicManagement: Crop rotation (3+ years).
Open row spacing for airflow.
Copper sprays at first symptoms.
Plant resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Rotation. Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on spinach leaf miner: serpentine pale tunnels between leaf surfaces. Multiple generations per year.
organicManagement: Row cover from emergence.
Remove and destroy mined leaves.
Parasitoid wasp habitat.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on spinach. Swiss chard is in the same family (Amaranthaceae) and hosts the same leaf miner species.
organicManagement: Row cover.
Sanitation.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Yellow patches on upper leaf surfaces with gray-purple downy growth on undersides; severe infections cause leaf collapse. Multiple races requiring continuous cultivar updates.
organicManagement: Plant downy-mildew-resistant cultivars (most modern hybrids carry race-specific resistance).
Open row spacing.
Avoid overhead irrigation late in the day.
Crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Broccoli is most vulnerable at transplant and first 4 weeks of growth.
organicManagement: Row cover from transplant.
Kaolin clay.
Trap crops.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Sudden wilting of lettuce heads with white cottony fungal growth at the soil line; black sclerotia in plant debris. Sclerotia survive 5+ years in soil.
organicManagement: Crop rotation (4+ years away from sclerotinia hosts).
Avoid overhead irrigation.
Open row spacing.
Remove infected plants immediately.
entries.prevention: Rotation. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Lower leaves of lettuce rot starting at the soil line; brown sunken lesions spread upward. Rhizoctonia solani; favored by wet soil conditions.
organicManagement: Open row spacing.
Crop rotation.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
Plant tolerant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Spacing. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on bush bean. Pole bean's longer growing window exposes it to more generations.
organicManagement: Row cover during early growth.
Time planting.
Pyrethrum on hot spots.
entries.prevention: Timing.

entries.symptoms: Yellow patches in fields; stunted growth, premature yellowing. Visible white-yellow cysts on roots in summer with a hand lens. SCN is the most damaging soybean pest worldwide.
organicManagement: Plant SCN-resistant cultivars; rotate resistance sources to avoid race shift.
Crop rotation (rotate to non-host crops; nematode populations decline 30-50 percent annually).
Cover crops with brassica biofumigants reduce nematode populations.
Soil testing to quantify nematode populations before planting.
entries.prevention: Resistance rotation. Soil testing.

entries.symptoms: Yellow patches between leaf veins becoming brown; severe infections cause pod abortion and yield loss of 30 percent or more. Fusarium virguliforme; soilborne.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars.
Avoid early planting in cold wet soils.
Sound crop rotation.
Improve field drainage.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Soil temperature at planting.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on green beans: yellow-orange beetle larvae skeletonize leaves. Edamame is in the soybean species; the bean beetle complex affects it identically.
organicManagement: Hand-pick adults and larvae.
Row cover during early growth.
Pyrethrum sprays during peak feeding.
Predator habitat for parasitic wasps.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on green beans. Lima bean is a primary Mexican bean beetle host.
organicManagement: Hand-pick.
Row cover.
Predator habitat.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Brown circular leaf lesions; severe infections cause leaf drop and yield loss. Cercospora arachidicola and Cercosporidium personatum; the dominant peanut foliar diseases.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars.
Sound crop rotation (3+ years away from peanut).
Copper sprays at first symptoms.
Bury residue to reduce overwintering spores.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Ring spots on leaves with yellow patterns; stunted plants; reduced pod set. Tomato spotted wilt virus, thrips-vectored.
organicManagement: Plant TSWV-resistant cultivars.
Manage thrips populations.
Reflective mulches.
Time planting to avoid peak thrips flights.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Thrips management.

entries.symptoms: Brown to black blotchy lesions on sweet potato skin; surface only, does not affect flesh. Cosmetic damage that reduces market quality.
organicManagement: Plant only certified disease-free slips.
Sound crop rotation.
Sanitation of crop residue.
Avoid storage at high humidity.
entries.prevention: Seed source. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard potato late blight. Yukon Gold is moderately susceptible; some races affect Yukon Gold more aggressively than other cultivars.
organicManagement: Copper sprays preventively during humid weather.
Open row spacing.
Sanitation of infected vines.
Sound crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard potato scab. Red Norland is moderately susceptible; appropriate soil pH management is essential.
organicManagement: Soil pH below 5.5.
Consistent irrigation.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: pH management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard garlic. Music's heritage hardneck genetics offer no special resistance; standard sanitation practices apply.
organicManagement: Certified nematode-free seed.
Hot-water treatment.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Certified seed.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard garlic.
organicManagement: Rotation.
Open spacing.
Sulfur sprays.
Sanitation.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as standard onion downy mildew. Walla Walla is moderately susceptible; cool wet PNW springs amplify pressure.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars where available.
Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Red Tropea is moderately susceptible.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
