Copper sprays.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Yellow tops of asparagus stems become reddish then dieback; crown wood becomes brown and soft. Soilborne; persists for many years. Reduces stand vigor over time.
organicManagement: Plant on raised beds for excellent drainage.
Plant in soil pH 6.5-7.5.
Plant Fusarium-resistant cultivars (Jersey series).
Sound site preparation; do not plant on former asparagus ground.
entries.prevention: Site selection. Cultivar.

entries.symptoms: Caterpillars tunnel through rhubarb stalks; wilted leaves and brown internal damage. Common cutworm-type pest.
organicManagement: Hand-knife removal from frass holes.
Bt sprays on early larval generations.
Sanitation of weeds and crop residue.
Fall tilling exposes pupae.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Brown circular leaf spots with concentric rings; severe infections cause leaf yellowing and reduced vigor.
organicManagement: Annual division and sanitation of old crowns.
Open row spacing.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
Copper sprays at first symptoms.
entries.prevention: Spacing. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Distorted fruit with deep scars and irregular ribbing at the blossom end; physiological disorder from cool temperatures during flower bud formation. Affects heirloom tomatoes most strongly.
organicManagement: Plant transplants after night temperatures reliably exceed 55F.
Use row covers during cool spring weather.
Plant cultivars resistant to catfacing where possible.
entries.prevention: Timing of transplant. Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on tomato. Pepper fruit develops dimpled white-yellow patches that turn corky.
organicManagement: Trap crops.
Hand-picking.
Spinosad on hot spots.
entries.prevention: Trap crops.

entries.symptoms: Brown circular leaf lesions with concentric rings; severe infections reduce head quality. Most damaging in warm humid conditions.
organicManagement: Crop rotation.
Open row spacing.
Copper sprays.
Sanitation of crop residue.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Sudden wilting and death of cucumber vines; bacterial ooze visible when stems are cut. Cucumber beetle-vectored. Different from solanum bacterial wilt.
organicManagement: Aggressive cucumber beetle management.
Row cover until first flower.
Plant resistant or tolerant cultivars.
Remove infected vines immediately.
entries.prevention: Cucumber beetle management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Broccoli is a primary host.
organicManagement: Bt and spinosad rotation.
Row cover.
Trichogramma releases.
entries.prevention: Monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Kale is a primary host with continuous pressure through fall and winter in mild zones.
organicManagement: Bt and spinosad rotation.
Row cover.
Trichogramma releases.
entries.prevention: Monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Tiny slender insects in flowers and leaf folds; vectors of TSWV and INSV virus. Silver scarring on leaves and fruit at oviposition sites.
organicManagement: Blue sticky traps for monitoring.
Insecticidal soap and neem on hot spots.
Predatory mite releases (Neoseiulus) in greenhouse production.
Reflective mulches deter thrips arrival.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring. Reflective mulch.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Pepper fruit develops silver scarring at oviposition sites; thrips also vector TSWV.
organicManagement: Blue sticky traps.
Insecticidal soap.
Predatory mites.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on onion. Garlic is a secondary thrips host; pressure is generally lower than on onion.
organicManagement: Reflective mulches.
Insecticidal soap.
entries.prevention: Reflective mulch.

entries.symptoms: Small psyllid insects suck plant sap and vector aster yellows phytoplasma. Severe infestations cause yellow witches'-broom carrot tops.
organicManagement: Row cover during early growth.
Insecticidal soap on hot spots.
Reflective mulches.
Encourage parasitic wasp populations.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard tomato: concentric brown leaf lesions. Cherry tomato's dense vine canopy traps humidity favoring blight.
organicManagement: Open trellising.
Sanitation of lower leaves.
Copper sprays.
Mulch heavily.
entries.prevention: Trellising. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard tomato: brown leathery patches at the blossom end. San Marzano paste-tomato types are particularly prone due to long fruit shape and calcium uptake patterns.
organicManagement: Mulch for consistent soil moisture.
Calcium chloride foliar sprays.
Avoid heavy nitrogen.
Maintain steady irrigation.
entries.prevention: Moisture consistency.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard tomato fusarium wilt. Roma paste tomatoes vary in resistance; many commercial Roma cultivars carry FF designation.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Rotation.
Grafting.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard tomato. Italian heirloom indeterminate types are typically more susceptible than modern hybrids; manage with open canopy and aggressive sanitation.
organicManagement: Open trellising for airflow.
Lower leaf removal as fruit ripens.
Copper sprays preventively.
Heavy mulch.
entries.prevention: Trellising. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Italian heirloom types have no specific late-blight resistance and require aggressive preventive management.
organicManagement: Copper sprays preventively during humid weather.
Open canopy.
Lower leaf removal.
Sanitation of infected foliage.
entries.prevention: Preventive copper schedule.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Eva Purple Ball is an heirloom with some reported late blight tolerance but no proven genetic resistance.
organicManagement: Copper sprays.
Open canopy.
entries.prevention: Preventive copper.

entries.symptoms: Dark concentric-ring lesions on stems at the soil line; severe infections cause stem dieback. Distinct from early blight; affects different tissues.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars.
Crop rotation.
Stake plants off the ground.
Heavy mulch.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Microscopic mites cluster on growing tips causing leaf distortion, bronzing, and bud blast. Severe infestations stunt plants. Often misdiagnosed as virus disease.
organicManagement: Sulfur dust on growing tips.
Insecticidal soap focused on terminal growth.
Predatory mites (Amblyseius swirskii) in greenhouse production.
Maintain plant vigor through proper fertility.
entries.prevention: Scout regularly with hand lens.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Pepper is highly susceptible; bud blast and leaf distortion can devastate crops.
organicManagement: Sulfur dust.
Insecticidal soap.
Predatory mites.
entries.prevention: Scouting.

entries.symptoms: Bright white pustules on leaf undersides; yellow patches on upper surfaces. Resembles downy mildew but with white rather than gray sporulation. Cool wet conditions favor.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cucumber. Squash species vary in susceptibility; cucurbita pepo is most affected.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
entries.prevention: Spacing.

entries.symptoms: Small light brown leaf spots with yellow halos; severe infections cause leaf yellowing and reduced root size.
organicManagement: Crop rotation.
Open row spacing.
Copper sprays.
Cultivar selection.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cabbage: green velvety caterpillars with irregular leaf holes. Brussels sprouts are a primary host; severe defoliation impacts sprout development.
organicManagement: Bt sprays at young larval stage.
Hand-pick larvae.
Row cover from transplant.
Trichogramma releases.
entries.prevention: Row cover. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on broccoli: pale yellow patches with downy growth on leaf undersides. Brussels sprouts in cool wet fall conditions are particularly susceptible.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Resistant cultivars where available.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cabbage. Kohlrabi's short season and exposed stem make leaf damage particularly visible.
organicManagement: Bt sprays.
Row cover.
Hand-picking.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Kohlrabi seedlings are vulnerable; established plants tolerate moderate damage.
organicManagement: Row cover from seeding.
Kaolin clay.
Trap crops of mustard.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Bok choy is a primary flea beetle host; severe damage reduces market quality significantly.
organicManagement: Row cover throughout the growing season.
Kaolin clay sprays.
Trap crops.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cabbage. Bok choy's loose head structure traps larvae and frass in stem crevices.
organicManagement: Bt sprays.
Row cover.
Hand-picking.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Napa cabbage's elongated head structure with loose outer leaves provides ample feeding sites for worms.
organicManagement: Bt sprays.
Row cover.
Hand-picking.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cabbage. Napa is particularly susceptible due to dense leafy structure trapping humidity.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Mizuna's fine leaves are heavily damaged by flea beetle pinhole feeding; aesthetic damage is visible immediately.
organicManagement: Row cover.
Trap crops.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Tatsoi's rosette habit traps larvae and droppings between leaves.
organicManagement: Bt sprays.
Row cover.
Hand-picking.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: White larvae tunnel through radish roots leaving brown trails; severe infestations cause root rot. Adult fly oviposits at the soil line around radish stems.
organicManagement: Row cover from seeding through harvest.
Beneficial nematodes drenched at the row.
Sound crop rotation away from brassicas.
Time spring planting to escape peak first-generation flight.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Daikon's long taproot is particularly vulnerable; larval tunnels can extend through the entire root.
organicManagement: Row cover.
Beneficial nematodes.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Turnip is highly susceptible.
organicManagement: Row cover.
Beneficial nematodes.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Swollen distorted root galls; plants wilt and yellow despite adequate moisture. Soilborne; persists 10+ years.
organicManagement: Soil pH above 7.0 (lime amendment).
Long crop rotation (7+ years).
Plant resistant cultivars where available.
Solarization in severely infested beds.
entries.prevention: pH management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on turnip. Rutabaga is highly susceptible.
organicManagement: Row cover.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Yellow ring patterns on leaves and fruit; fruit develops bumpy distorted skin; stunted growth and reduced yield. Aphid-vectored; devastating in commercial papaya production.
organicManagement: Plant transgenic ringspot-resistant cultivars (commercial Hawaii production).
Aphid management with insecticidal soap.
Reflective mulches.
Remove infected plants immediately.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Aphid management.
entries.source: USDA-ARS Pacific Basin Agricultural Research Center

entries.symptoms: Female fly oviposits in young fruit; larvae tunnel through flesh causing premature drop. Multiple generations per year.
organicManagement: Fruit bagging at golf-ball size.
Spinosad bait sprays.
Sanitation of fallen fruit.
Pheromone trap monitoring.
entries.prevention: Bagging.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as papaya fruit fly. Bactrocera correcta and related species cause severe damage; tropical fruit fly complex requires aggressive management.
organicManagement: Fruit bagging.
Spinosad bait sprays.
Sanitation.
Trap monitoring.
entries.prevention: Bagging.

entries.symptoms: White waxy clusters at leaf bases and on fruit; severe infestations vector pineapple mealybug wilt virus (PMWV). Causes leaf yellowing and plant collapse.
organicManagement: Aggressive ant management; ants tend mealybugs.
Insecticidal soap on hot spots.
Encourage parasitic wasps.
Remove infested plants.
entries.prevention: Ant management.

entries.symptoms: Female fly oviposits in olive fruit; larvae tunnel through flesh causing premature drop and quality loss. Tan dimpled spots on green fruit indicate oviposition.
organicManagement: Yellow-and-protein bait traps (Olipe traps) for monitoring and mass-trapping.
Spinosad bait sprays during peak adult flight.
Kaolin clay sprays from fruit set through harvest.
Sanitation of fallen fruit.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Knobby galls on stems and twigs; spread through pruning wounds and frost cracks. Pseudomonas savastanoi.
organicManagement: Prune in dry weather only.
Sterilize tools between cuts.
Copper sprays after rainy weather.
Remove and destroy galled wood.
entries.prevention: Pruning timing. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Asymmetric yellowing of leaves; misshapen bitter fruit; eventual tree death. Bacterial disease (Candidatus Liberibacter), Asian citrus psyllid-vectored. Devastating to commercial citrus.
organicManagement: Plant only certified disease-free nursery stock.
Aggressive psyllid management with kaolin clay and insecticidal soap.
Remove and destroy infected trees.
Cover small trees with insect netting.
entries.prevention: Cultivar source. Psyllid management.

entries.symptoms: Raised brown lesions with yellow halos on leaves and fruit. Xanthomonas citri; spreads in wind-driven rain.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars where available.
Copper sprays during humid weather.
Sanitation: remove fallen fruit and prune wounded branches.
Maintain wind barriers.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Copper sprays.

entries.symptoms: Tiny brown-orange psyllids on new growth; sometimes visible as small jumpers when disturbed. Primary vector of citrus greening; presence alone warrants aggressive management.
organicManagement: Kaolin clay sprays on new flush.
Insecticidal soap.
Cover small trees with insect netting.
Quarantine compliance in affected areas.
entries.prevention: Insect netting. Quarantine.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Key lime is highly susceptible.
organicManagement: Disease-free stock.
Psyllid management.
Sanitation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar source.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Bearss lime is moderately susceptible.
organicManagement: Cultivar selection.
Copper sprays.
Sanitation.
entries.prevention: Copper sprays.

entries.symptoms: Yellow-orange spore pustules on leaf undersides; severe infections cause premature leaf drop. Most damaging in humid summers.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Open canopy pruning.
Sulfur sprays during humid weather.
Fall leaf cleanup.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Yellow mosaic patterns on leaves; reduced vigor. Mite-vectored. Most trees remain productive despite infection.
organicManagement: Plant virus-tested nursery stock.
Manage mite populations.
Maintain vigor through proper irrigation and fertility.
entries.prevention: Cultivar source.

entries.symptoms: Internal fruit rot at the calyx end; external symptoms may be minimal until late stage. Various fungal pathogens enter through cracking during humid weather.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars (some Russian/Caucasian varieties show better resistance).
Open canopy pruning.
Mulch to maintain consistent moisture.
Harvest before splitting.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Brown lesions on fruit and leaves with concentric rings; severe infections cause fruit drop. Most damaging in humid summers.
organicManagement: Open canopy pruning.
Copper sprays.
Sanitation of crop residue.
Resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Bacterial canker (Pseudomonas syringae pv. actinidiae); rust-colored ooze from infected vines; sudden vine collapse. Particularly damaging to commercial production worldwide.
organicManagement: Copper sprays at petal fall and during humid weather.
Prune in dry weather; sterilize tools.
Plant resistant cultivars (Hayward is highly susceptible; Saanichton 12 has some resistance).
Remove infected vines immediately.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Brown circular leaf spots; cosmetic damage that rarely affects fruit production or tree vigor.
organicManagement: Accept cosmetic damage on home plantings.
Annual leaf cleanup.
Open canopy pruning.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on apple and pear. Quince is highly susceptible to fire blight; the species sometimes named in the disease (Erwinia amylovora named in quince trials).
organicManagement: Aggressive copper schedule during bloom.
Pruning out infections in dry weather.
Sterilize tools between cuts.
entries.prevention: Reconsider quince in fire-blight-prone regions.

entries.symptoms: Flowering and seeding caused by heat and long-day photoperiod, not by pathogen. Affected plants are unmarketable as fresh herb but useful for coriander seed production.
organicManagement: Plant slow-bolt cultivars (Santo, Calypso).
Time plantings to cooler windows: early spring and fall.
Shade with afternoon cover in summer plantings.
Use for coriander seed if bolting occurs.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on other crops. Cilantro is moderately susceptible in humid late-summer conditions.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Open row spacing.
Avoid overhead watering.
entries.prevention: Spacing.

entries.symptoms: Septoria leaf spot causes small brown leaf spots with darker margins; severe infections defoliate plants.
organicManagement: Crop rotation.
Open row spacing.
Copper sprays.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Mottled yellow-green leaves with deformed and reduced fruit. Mechanically transmitted through tools and tobacco residue.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars (Tm-1, Tm-2 designations).
Wash hands and tools before handling.
Avoid tobacco use in or near tomato beds.
Remove infected plants.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection. Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Mottled leaves; stunted plants; reduced fruit production. Mechanically transmitted.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars.
Sanitation of tools and hands.
Remove infected plants.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on watermelon: brown circular lesions with rings on leaves and fruit. Most damaging in humid conditions.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on watermelon fusarium wilt. Cantaloupe is highly susceptible.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Rotation.
Grafting.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cucumber. Cantaloupe is highly susceptible in humid regions.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Winter squash fruit develops circular brown lesions; storage rot follows.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Sound rotation.
Cool dry storage.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic.
organicManagement: Hand-picking.
Trap crops.
Spinosad.
entries.prevention: Trap crops.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on tomato early blight. Potato is a primary host; defoliation reduces tuber yield.
organicManagement: Mulch heavily.
Crop rotation.
Copper sprays.
Plant resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on other crops: wilting on one side of the plant; brown vascular discoloration in stems. Soilborne; persists 10+ years.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Long crop rotation.
Solarization.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Eggplant is highly susceptible.
organicManagement: Resistant rootstock grafting.
Long rotation.
Solarization.
entries.prevention: Grafting.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on potato. Eggplant is a primary host; pressure builds quickly.
organicManagement: Hand-pick adults, larvae, and egg masses daily.
Bt tenebrionis (potato beetle-specific) sprays.
Spinosad on resistant populations.
Beauveria bassiana fungal sprays.
entries.prevention: Daily monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on broccoli. Cool wet weather favors infection; kale's long growing season exposes it to multiple infection windows.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Wilting and yellowing; brown vascular discoloration in lower stem. Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. lactucae; soilborne. Race-specific resistance is available.
organicManagement: Plant race-specific resistant cultivars.
Long crop rotation.
Solarization.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cucumber. Pumpkin is highly susceptible in humid regions.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard onion fusarium: leaf yellowing; bulb basal plate softens and rots. Soilborne; persists for years.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars.
Long crop rotation.
Avoid wounding bulbs at harvest.
Solarization.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Green peach aphid clusters on undersides of spinach leaves; vectors multiple viruses including beet curly top.
organicManagement: Reflective mulches.
Insecticidal soap.
Encourage parasitic wasp populations.
entries.prevention: Reflective mulch.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Celery is a green peach aphid host.
organicManagement: Reflective mulches.
Insecticidal soap.
entries.prevention: Reflective mulch.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Green peach aphid vectors CMV on cucumber.
organicManagement: Reflective mulches.
Insecticidal soap.
Predator habitat.
entries.prevention: Reflective mulch.

entries.symptoms: Yellow-orange spore pustules on leaf undersides; severe infections cause premature leaf drop. Alternate host is hemlock.
organicManagement: Maintain distance from hemlock plantings.
Sulfur sprays during humid weather.
Open spacing.
Resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Stunted bushy growth with crumbly distorted fruit; reduced yield and quality. Pollen-transmitted; spreads rapidly through commercial plantings.
organicManagement: Plant certified virus-tested stock.
Remove infected canes.
Source from disease-free nurseries.
Plant tolerant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Stock source.

entries.symptoms: Adult weevils feed on leaf margins creating crescent notches; larvae feed on roots causing plant decline. Severe infestations kill plants.

Hand-pick adults at dusk.
Sound crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Nematodes annually.

entries.symptoms: Outer leaves wilt and turn brown; inner leaves remain green. Soilborne; persists 10+ years.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars.
Long crop rotation away from nightshades and strawberry.
Solarization.
Soil testing for verticillium populations before planting.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on leek. Garlic is a secondary leek moth host in regions where leek moth is established.
organicManagement: Row cover.
Bt sprays.
Sound crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Small moth larvae mine through leaves creating window-pane damage, then bore into fruit. Primarily a southern US tomato pest.
organicManagement: Bt sprays.
Pheromone trap monitoring.
Remove and destroy infested fruit.
Encourage parasitic wasps.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as tomato hornworm. Pepper is a secondary host; tomato hornworm and tobacco hornworm both feed on pepper.
organicManagement: Hand-pick.
Bt sprays.
Parasitic wasps.
entries.prevention: Scouting.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Cabbage looper is one of three primary kale caterpillars (with cabbage worm and diamondback moth).
organicManagement: Bt sprays.
Hand-pick.
Row cover.
Trichogramma.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Collards in southern US production are continuously exposed to looper pressure.
organicManagement: Bt sprays.
Row cover.
Trichogramma.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Lettuce is a secondary cabbage looper host; pressure rises after primary brassica hosts are removed.
organicManagement: Bt sprays.
Row cover.
Hand-pick.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Pink Lady's exceptionally long hang time (October-November) exposes fruit to all moth generations.
organicManagement: Granulosis virus.
Pheromone mating disruption.
Kaolin clay weekly.
Bagging.
entries.prevention: Trap monitoring.
entries.source: WSU Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Northern Spy is highly scab-susceptible; one of the standard susceptible heritage cultivars.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Fall leaf cleanup.
Open canopy.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. McIntosh is among the most scab-susceptible commercial cultivars.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at 7-day intervals.
Annual fall leaf cleanup.
Open canopy.
Consider replanting with resistant cultivar in heavy-scab regions.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Cortland is highly scab-susceptible.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Sanitation.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Empire is moderately susceptible.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Sanitation.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Rome is moderately susceptible.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Sanitation.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: White powdery growth on new shoots and leaves. Liberty has scab resistance but is moderately susceptible to powdery mildew.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays at 7-10 day intervals during active growth.
Prune infected shoots in summer.
Open canopy.
entries.prevention: Sulfur schedule.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on winter wheat. Spring wheat is exposed to leaf rust spores moving northward each summer.
organicManagement: Resistant cultivars.
Volunteer wheat removal.
Sulfur sprays on small acreage.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on tomato. Pepper blossom end rot follows the same calcium-uptake disorder.
organicManagement: Mulch.
Consistent irrigation.
Calcium foliar sprays.
entries.prevention: Moisture.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Watermelon blossom end rot is more cosmetic than tomato due to thicker rind.
organicManagement: Mulch.
Consistent irrigation.
entries.prevention: Moisture.

entries.symptoms: Pale yellow aphids on stems and leaves; severe infestations cause leaf curl, sticky honeydew, sooty mold, and vector soybean mosaic virus.
organicManagement: Encourage natural enemy populations (ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps).
Plant tolerant cultivars where available.
Insecticidal soap on hot spots.
Time planting to avoid peak migration.
entries.prevention: Predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cucumber. Winter squash is a primary host; vectors bacterial wilt.
organicManagement: Row cover.
Trap crops of Hubbard.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Pumpkin is a primary host.
organicManagement: Row cover.
Trap crops.
entries.prevention: Row cover.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on turnip clubroot. Broccoli roots develop swollen distorted galls; plants wilt despite watering.
organicManagement: Soil pH above 7.0.
Long rotation (7+ years).
Resistant cultivars.
Solarization.
entries.prevention: pH management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic.
organicManagement: Soil pH above 7.0.
Long rotation.
Resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: pH management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on raspberry: purple-rimmed cane lesions; severe infections weaken canes. Most damaging in humid sites.
organicManagement: Lime sulfur dormant spray.
Open row spacing.
Crop rotation.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Cranberry fruitworm larvae feed inside developing blueberries causing premature ripening, drop, and silk webbing. Distinct from blueberry maggot; smaller larvae.
organicManagement: Pheromone trap monitoring.
Spinosad sprays at egg hatch.
Drop berry sanitation.
entries.prevention: Monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on onion downy mildew. Shallot is highly susceptible.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Copper sprays.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Leek is moderately susceptible.
organicManagement: Spacing.
Copper sprays.
Rotation.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Resistant cultivars.
Open spacing.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. White powdery growth on leaves; less common on pepper than on cucurbits or tomatoes.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Open spacing.
Resistant cultivars.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: White powdery growth on leaves and stems; severe infections cause leaf yellowing and reduced bloom quality. Zinnia is highly susceptible.
organicManagement: Plant powdery-mildew-resistant cultivars (Profusion, Zahara series).
Open spacing for airflow.
Sulfur sprays at 7-10 day intervals.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Moth larvae feed in developing sunflower heads, tunneling through seeds and frass. Severe infestations destroy harvestable seed yield.
organicManagement: Pheromone trap monitoring for first flight.
Bt sprays at first egg hatch.
Time plantings to avoid peak adult flight.
Sanitation of crop residue.
entries.prevention: Timing. Monitoring.

entries.symptoms: Orange spore pustules on leaves; severe infections reduce seed yield. Most damaging in late season.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars.
Crop rotation.
Open spacing.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Japanese beetle adults feed on marigold foliage and flowers. Marigold can serve as a trap crop drawing beetles from primary vegetable beds.
organicManagement: Hand-pick adults from marigold trap plantings.
Accept marigold damage as part of trap crop strategy.
Milky spore on adjacent lawn for long-term grub suppression.
entries.prevention: Trap crop strategy.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on carrot: green or distorted flowers, yellow witches'-broom growth. Leafhopper-vectored phytoplasma; affects many composite flowers.
organicManagement: Manage leafhopper populations with reflective mulches.
Remove infected plants.
Avoid planting near infected lettuce or aster crops.
entries.prevention: Leafhopper management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. White powdery growth on calendula leaves; moderate infections affect appearance more than flower production.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Open spacing.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
entries.prevention: Spacing.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Aphids cluster on nasturtium leaves and stems; nasturtium serves as a deliberate aphid trap crop.
organicManagement: Accept aphid concentration as trap crop function.
Insecticidal soap on heavy infestations near valuable crops.
Encourage predators (ladybugs, lacewings) at trap site.
entries.prevention: Trap crop strategy.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. White powdery growth on borage leaves; affects appearance but rarely reduces flower production.
organicManagement: Sulfur sprays.
Open spacing.
entries.prevention: Spacing.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Bee balm is highly susceptible to powdery mildew; severe infections defoliate plants.
organicManagement: Plant powdery-mildew-resistant cultivars (Jacob Cline, Marshall's Delight).
Open spacing.
Divide established clumps annually to reduce density.
Sulfur sprays.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on cosmos. Echinacea flowers become green and distorted (witches'-broom); affected plants are non-recovering.
organicManagement: Remove and destroy infected plants.
Manage leafhopper populations.
Plant resistant cultivars where available.
entries.prevention: Leafhopper management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Phacelia is moderately attractive to aphids; trap-crop function balances damage.
organicManagement: Accept moderate populations.
Encourage predators.
entries.prevention: Predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Yarrow is moderately resistant; powdery mildew is rarely a serious problem in well-spaced plantings.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
Divide clumps every 3-4 years.
entries.prevention: Spacing.

entries.symptoms: Orange spore pustules on leaf undersides; severe infections cause leaf drop. Affects mostly common comfrey; Bocking 14 cultivar shows better resistance.
organicManagement: Plant resistant cultivars (Bocking 14, Bocking 4).
Remove and destroy infected leaves.
Open spacing.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Cercospora leaf spot causes small brown circular lesions on leaves; severe infections cause defoliation.
organicManagement: Open spacing.
Crop rotation.
Copper sprays.
entries.prevention: Rotation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic as on standard basil. Tulsi (holy basil) shows somewhat more tolerance than Genovese basil but is still susceptible.
organicManagement: Plant in well-ventilated locations.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
Remove infected plants.
entries.prevention: Cultivar selection.

entries.symptoms: Orange spore pustules on leaf undersides; minor damage that rarely affects root production.
organicManagement: Accept minor damage.
Sound rotation.
Open spacing.
entries.prevention: Spacing.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Medicinal echinacea is highly susceptible; root production is reduced or eliminated by infection.
organicManagement: Remove infected plants.
Manage leafhopper populations.
Plant resistant cultivars where available.
entries.prevention: Leafhopper management.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Feverfew is in the Asteraceae family and is moderately susceptible to aster yellows.
organicManagement: Remove infected plants.
Manage leafhoppers.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Black aphids cluster on milk thistle stems and leaves; severe infestations weaken plants but rarely affect seed production.
organicManagement: Encourage ladybug populations.
Insecticidal soap on heavy infestations.
Accept moderate populations.
entries.prevention: Predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Brown leaf spots on stinging nettle; rare and rarely significant.
organicManagement: Accept minor damage.
Cut and remove infected stems.
entries.prevention: Sanitation.

entries.symptoms: Same diagnostic. Chamomile is moderately aphid-attractive; rarely causes serious damage.
organicManagement: Strong water spray.
Insecticidal soap.
Encourage predators.
entries.prevention: Predator habitat.

entries.symptoms: Pink or green, pear-shaped aphids 2 to 4 mm long with long legs and antennae, clustered on growing tips and undersides of upper leaves. Honeydew and sooty mold below. Curled, distorted new growth. Vectors potato virus Y and potato leafroll virus.
organicManagement: Conserve natural enemies: lady beetles, lacewings, syrphid larvae, parasitic wasps suppress populations below action thresholds in unsprayed gardens.
Spot-treat hot spots with insecticidal soap (1 to 2%) covering undersides of leaves; repeat after 5 to 7 days if needed.
Apply horticultural oil at 1 to 2% on dormant or summer rates against early colonies; avoid hot afternoons.
Strong water spray dislodges colonies on tomato and pepper; aphids do not climb back efficiently.
entries.prevention: Avoid excessive nitrogen, which fuels aphid reproduction. Inspect undersides of upper leaves weekly from early flowering. Remove and destroy heavily infested shoots before populations explode.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UC IPM Online

entries.symptoms: Tiny, soft-bodied aphids 1 to 2 mm long, varying yellow to dark green nearly black, clustered on undersides of leaves. Causes leaf curl, stunting, honeydew, and sooty mold. Vectors cucumber mosaic virus, watermelon mosaic virus, and zucchini yellow mosaic virus.
organicManagement: Conserve aphid predators: lady beetles, lacewings, syrphid larvae, and parasitic wasps (Lysiphlebus testaceipes) provide season-long control.
Spray insecticidal soap on infested foliage; coverage of leaf undersides is critical.
Apply horticultural or neem oil at 1 to 2% for resistant colonies; respect heat and re-entry intervals.
Use reflective silver mulch under cucurbit transplants; repels colonizing winged aphids and delays virus arrival.
entries.prevention: Plant cucurbit transplants away from overwintered weed reservoirs (mallow, lambsquarters, pigweed). Avoid heavy spring nitrogen. Scout fields twice weekly during early vine run.
entries.source: UC IPM Online; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.symptoms: Bluish green aphids 1 to 2 mm long with short cornicles, packed into the whorl and tassel of corn and the boot of sorghum. Honeydew can prevent pollen shedding. Vectors barley yellow dwarf virus and maize dwarf mosaic virus.
organicManagement: Lacewing eggs (Chrysoperla rufilabris) released at 1,000 per acre at tassel initiation can suppress whorl colonies.
Encourage syrphid flies with field-edge sweet alyssum or buckwheat insectary strips.
For sweet corn home gardens, water blast tassels and whorls early morning to dislodge colonies before silking.
entries.prevention: Plant early, since the worst damage coincides with late-planted whorl-stage corn meeting peak aphid migration. Maintain plant health to compensate for sub-economic feeding.

entries.symptoms: Shiny green aphids 2 to 3 mm long with a dark spot at the base of each cornicle. Strong toxic feeding response: leaves yellow and pucker dramatically even at low aphid densities. Common in cool greenhouses and high tunnels. Vectors potato leafroll virus.
organicManagement: Release parasitoid wasps (Aphidius ervi) at 0.5 wasps per square meter weekly in greenhouses and tunnels.
Apply insecticidal soap to leaf undersides; pay attention to growing tips where colonies concentrate.
Foxglove aphid migrates quickly when disturbed; repeated low-impact sprays are more effective than a single heavy application.
entries.prevention: Inspect transplants for hitchhikers before moving into protected culture. Manage tunnel temperatures between 18 and 22 C if possible; foxglove aphid populations explode below 15 C.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; Penn State Extension

entries.symptoms: Gray-green aphids 1 to 2 mm long, lightly waxy, in dense colonies on flower stalks and undersides of mature leaves. Distinct from cabbage aphid by less pronounced wax. Particularly damaging on turnip greens and mustard.
organicManagement: Strip-spray hot spots with insecticidal soap; thorough coverage of leaf undersides and bolt stalks.
Encourage parasitoid wasps (Diaeretiella rapae) by leaving a small unsprayed strip with flowering brassica residue.
Yellow sticky cards near transplants help intercept fall-flight winged colonizers.
entries.prevention: Time fall plantings to avoid the September aphid flight peak in southern regions. Remove and destroy bolt stalks from spring brassicas before they release winged generations.

entries.symptoms: Yellow-green aphids 1 to 2 mm long with dark cornicles, clustered on new shoot growth. Causes curled, distorted apple and citrus shoots. Honeydew supports sooty mold. Vectors citrus tristeza virus.
organicManagement: Conserve natural enemies; lady beetle larvae and syrphid larvae clean up colonies in 2 to 3 weeks of warm weather.
Dormant horticultural oil application targets overwintering eggs on apple and citrus.
Spot-treat heavy colonies with insecticidal soap during shoot flush.
entries.prevention: Avoid excessive spring nitrogen on young trees, which produces susceptible shoots. Prune to open the canopy; predators reach colonies faster in well-lit interiors.

entries.symptoms: Pale green aphids 1 to 2 mm long with a finger-like projection above the cauda. Curls carrot and parsnip foliage. Most damaging as the vector of carrot motley dwarf, a complex of two viruses (carrot mottle and carrot redleaf) that yellows and stunts the crop.
organicManagement: Floating row cover from emergence through canopy closure excludes the winged colonizers.
Spot-treat colonies with insecticidal soap once row cover is removed for harvest scouting.
Encourage syrphid flies with adjacent flowering dill, cilantro, and sweet alyssum.
entries.prevention: Separate carrot plantings from willow windbreaks where the aphid overwinters. Do not plant carrots adjacent to umbelliferous weed reservoirs (Queen Anne's lace, wild parsnip).

entries.symptoms: Purplish-pink aphids 2 to 3 mm long, lightly waxy, that curl new apple shoots into tight rosettes. Even brief feeding at tight cluster through petal fall causes lasting fruit deformation: small, gnarled, knobby apples that persist on the tree at harvest. The most economically damaging apple aphid.
organicManagement: Delayed dormant horticultural oil at 2% targets overwintered eggs before bud break and is the single most effective organic intervention.
Conserve natural enemies; lady beetles, syrphid larvae, and parasitic wasps (Ephedrus, Aphidius) clean up summer colonies.
Plantain (Plantago major), the summer host, should not be tolerated in apple drives or under-tree strips.
entries.prevention: Apply dormant oil annually at half-inch green tip on susceptible cultivars. Manage plantain in orchard floor. Scout pink bud through petal fall; once fruit deformation begins it cannot be reversed.

entries.symptoms: Microscopic mites under 0.3 mm requiring 20x magnification to see. Hidden in crowns and unopened leaves. Symptoms: stunted, distorted new growth; blackened, curled leaves; aborted or distorted flowers; stunted runners on strawberry. Damage often precedes detection.
organicManagement: Release predatory mites (Neoseiulus cucumeris or Amblyseius swirskii) at 50 to 100 per plant for greenhouse and tunnel crops.
Hot water dip of strawberry transplants at 44 C for 7 minutes kills mites inside the crown without killing the plant.
Remove and destroy heavily infested crowns; this mite does not crawl far between plants.
entries.prevention: Buy certified mite-free strawberry transplants from reputable nurseries. Quarantine new ornamental introductions for two weeks. Avoid overhead irrigation, which spreads mites between plants.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension; UF/IFAS Featured Creatures

entries.symptoms: Red to brick-red mites 0.4 mm long with prominent white setae on the back, on undersides of apple, pear, and stone fruit leaves. Feeding bronzes leaves silver-gray, then yellow, then brown. Heavy populations defoliate trees, reducing return bloom and fruit size.
organicManagement: Delayed dormant horticultural oil at 2% at half-inch green tip smothers overwintering eggs (visible as tiny red dots in twig crotches).
Conserve predatory mites Typhlodromus pyri and Amblyseius andersoni by avoiding broad-spectrum insecticides through the season.
Summer horticultural oil at 1% suppresses summer generations; respect temperature and dryness restrictions on the label.
entries.prevention: Annual dormant oil is the cornerstone of organic management on apple. Build and conserve predatory mite populations over 2 to 3 seasons; once established, T. pyri provides season-long biocontrol.

entries.symptoms: Pearly white, slow-moving, shiny mites 0.5 to 1 mm long, in clusters under bulb scales and on basal plates of onion, garlic, and ornamental bulbs. Usually secondary on tissue already damaged by Fusarium, nematodes, or mechanical injury. Severe infestations rot the bulb in storage.
organicManagement: Cure bulbs at 30 C with low humidity for 7 to 14 days before storage; populations crash under dry conditions.
Hot water treatment of garlic seed at 49 C for 20 minutes kills mites before planting.
Discard and destroy soft or weeping bulbs; do not compost in proximity to next year's planting stock.
entries.prevention: Plant only firm, undamaged seed cloves and bulbs. Rotate alliums away from infested fields for 3 to 4 years. Improve drainage; bulb mites thrive in saturated soil.

entries.symptoms: Pale yellow-green mites 0.4 mm long with two faint dark spots, on undersides of grape, almond, and stone fruit leaves. Heavier feeder than two-spotted mite; causes rapid leaf bronzing and shed in hot dry conditions. Webbing visible on heavily infested shoots.
organicManagement: Release predatory mites Galendromus occidentalis at 1,000 per acre at first detection in vineyards and orchards.
Maintain orchard floor cover crops to support predator reservoirs through the season.
Apply sulfur dust at petal fall and through the season for grape; serves dual function against powdery mildew.
Summer horticultural oil at 1% with predator-safe formulations only.
entries.prevention: Avoid dusty conditions, which strongly favor mite outbreaks. Manage road and ranch dust with cover crops and irrigation timing. Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that flare Pacific mite populations.

entries.symptoms: Microscopic eriophyid mites (under 0.2 mm) invisible without a hand lens. Diagnostic: small (3 to 4 mm) reddish-to-black blister-like spots on upper and lower leaf surfaces of pear in spring, sometimes coalescing into large necrotic patches. Heavily attacked leaves drop; fruit may carry surface russeting.
organicManagement: Delayed dormant lime sulfur application at silver tip (5 gallons per 100) kills overwintering mites under bud scales.
Summer infestations rarely warrant treatment; affected leaves drop and replacement leaves emerge clean.
Sulfur dust during early shoot flush suppresses spring colonization.
entries.prevention: Annual delayed dormant lime sulfur on pear plantings with known history. Inspect new pear nursery stock for blistered leaves before purchase.

entries.symptoms: Red to red-brown mites 0.75 mm long with characteristic very long front legs giving an 'antenna' appearance. Common around foundations of houses in spring and fall as they migrate from lawns. Feeds on grass and clover; rarely causes significant plant damage but stains walls and curtains when crushed indoors.
organicManagement: Remove vegetation in an 18 to 24 inch band against the foundation; clover mites will not cross bare gravel or mulch.
Indoors, vacuum mites rather than crushing them to avoid red staining.
Diatomaceous earth in foundation cracks and weep holes provides a physical barrier.
entries.prevention: Maintain a mite-free foundation buffer year-round. Repair window screens and door sweeps to exclude indoor migration.

entries.symptoms: Tan to fawn-colored beetles 10 to 12 mm long with long spiny legs. Skeletonize foliage and flower petals in dense feeding aggregations during a 4 to 6 week emergence period in late spring. Larvae are white grubs in sandy soil. Especially destructive on grape, raspberry, and rose.
organicManagement: Handpick beetles into soapy water in early morning when they are sluggish; check daily during emergence flight.
Floating row cover over vulnerable shoots through emergence flight (typically 6 weeks ending early July).
Kaolin clay (Surround WP) at 50 lb per acre as repellent protectant on grape and raspberry.
Note: beetles contain a cardiac toxin (cantharidin-like compound) that is acutely toxic to poultry; do not feed handpicked beetles to chickens.
entries.prevention: Avoid siting new fruit plantings near sandy areas (golf courses, beaches, sand banks) where larvae develop. Cover crops on sandy fields reduce larval habitat.

entries.symptoms: Adults: dark brown to reddish-brown beetles 12 to 25 mm long, attracted to lights in late spring and early summer; chew ragged holes in foliage of oak, fruit trees, and ornamentals. Larvae: white grubs with C-shape posture, 25 to 40 mm long at maturity, in turf and garden soil where they feed on roots for 1 to 3 years.
organicManagement: Beneficial nematodes (Heterorhabditis bacteriophora) drenched into moist soil at 1 billion per acre target grubs.
Encourage skunks, moles, and ground beetles as larval predators; tolerate their digging in mid-summer.
Adult beetles can be handpicked from foliage at night by flashlight and dropped into soapy water; not practical for large infestations.
entries.prevention: Rotate vegetable beds away from former lawn for at least one season; converted lawn beds carry the highest grub populations. Maintain lawn at 3 inches or taller, which reduces female beetle oviposition success.

entries.symptoms: Adults: large velvety green beetles 20 to 25 mm long with bronze-yellow side stripes, day-flying. Feed on ripe and over-ripe fruit (figs, peaches, blackberries), often entering through bird-pecked openings. Larvae: large white grubs 40 to 50 mm long that move on their backs through manure-rich turf and compost; rarely damage healthy roots but disrupt turf surface.
organicManagement: Bag fruit clusters (peach, fig) with paper or fine mesh bags 2 weeks before ripening to exclude adults.
Harvest ripe fruit promptly; do not allow over-ripe fruit to fall and accumulate.
Beneficial nematodes for larval suppression in heavily affected turf adjacent to gardens.
Reduce compost addition under high-value fruit trees if adult populations are high; concentrated compost is a strong larval attractant.
entries.prevention: Limit summer composting depth under fruit trees. Pick up windfall promptly. Encourage native birds as adult predators.

entries.symptoms: Adults: elongate, somewhat flat beetles 10 to 20 mm long that snap audibly and flip into the air when placed on their back. Mostly harmless as adults. Larvae are wireworms (slender, segmented, copper-colored, 15 to 40 mm) that bore into seed pieces, tubers, and roots underground. Adults useful as a diagnostic for hidden wireworm pressure.
organicManagement: Bait trapping with carrot or potato pieces buried 10 cm deep and excavated after 7 days assesses wireworm populations before planting.
Solarization of beds for 6 to 8 weeks in summer reduces both adults and larvae.
Avoid planting potato or root crops into newly broken sod for at least one season; wireworms persist 2 to 4 years.
entries.prevention: Crop rotation away from grasses and small grains, which favor wireworm reproduction. Maintain low organic matter in heavy-pressure beds for 1 to 2 seasons.

entries.symptoms: Bright scarlet-red adults 6 to 9 mm long with black legs and head, easily spotted on true lilies (Lilium spp.) and fritillaries. Larvae are humpbacked grubs that carry their own excrement on their backs as a defense; cause heavy defoliation. A serious invasive in NE US since 1992. Does NOT feed on daylilies (Hemerocallis).
organicManagement: Handpick adults daily during spring and early summer; drop into soapy water. Adults play dead and drop; hold a container underneath.
Remove orange egg masses (laid in rows on leaf undersides) and crush larvae before they reach reproductive maturity.
Neem oil spray on small larvae; reapply weekly through the larval period.
entries.prevention: Avoid moving Lilium and Fritillaria nursery stock from infested regions. Inspect new lily bulbs and potted plants for adults and eggs before planting.

entries.symptoms: Soft-bodied beetles 10 to 25 mm long with elongate cylindrical body, narrow neck, and large head. Color varies by species: black, gray, striped, or with red and yellow markings. Aggregate in destructive groups on tomato, potato, alfalfa, and many vegetable crops. Contain cantharidin, a blistering agent toxic to livestock if ingested in alfalfa hay.
organicManagement: Handpick beetles wearing gloves (cantharidin can blister bare skin); drop into soapy water. Knock beetles into a bucket using a stick.
Floating row cover during outbreak periods (usually mid-summer following grasshopper years; blister beetle larvae are grasshopper egg predators).
Kaolin clay protectant on tomato and potato during outbreak periods.
entries.prevention: Manage grasshopper populations in adjacent rangeland; blister beetle outbreaks track grasshopper egg abundance. Inspect alfalfa hay before feeding to horses.

entries.symptoms: Yellow-green beetles 5 to 6 mm long with three transverse green bands across the wing covers (the lateral bands often appear as spots). Adults feed on cucurbit, bean, and lettuce foliage and flowers; larvae feed on roots of corn and grasses. A southern US counterpart to spotted cucumber beetle, distinguished by the banded pattern.
organicManagement: Floating row cover on cucurbit transplants until female flowers open; remove for pollination.
Trap crop of blue Hubbard squash planted 7 to 10 days before main planting concentrates beetle pressure.
Kaolin clay spray on cucurbits and beans during peak migration.
Beneficial nematodes for larval suppression in corn fields with high adult pressure.
entries.prevention: Crop rotation between cucurbits and unrelated crops (small grains, brassicas). Eliminate weedy field edges that harbor adults.

entries.symptoms: Small (5 to 6 mm) flattened oval beetles, pale tan to yellow-gold with metallic sheen, that look like tiny turtles. Larvae are spiny and carry an 'umbrella' of dried excrement on a forked tail. Feed on eggplant foliage causing characteristic small round holes throughout the leaf.
organicManagement: Handpick adults and larvae; both are slow-moving and easy to remove.
Floating row cover on transplants for the first 4 weeks after transplant.
Tolerate light damage; mature eggplant withstands significant defoliation without yield loss.
entries.prevention: Crop rotation away from solanaceous crops on a 3-year cycle. Remove and destroy crop residue after harvest.

entries.symptoms: Bright red snout beetles 6 mm long with black snout, legs, and underside. Females drill into rose buds with their long snout and lay eggs inside; buds fail to open and may drop. Larvae develop inside the bud, then drop to soil to pupate. Also damages rose hips.
organicManagement: Handpick adults from rose foliage and buds in the morning; drop into soapy water.
Remove and destroy aborted buds and rose hips that may harbor developing larvae; do not compost.
Cultivate soil under rose plantings in early spring to disrupt overwintering pupae.
entries.prevention: Inspect rose nursery stock before purchase. Maintain clean cultivation under rose beds through the season.

entries.symptoms: Bright red snout beetles 6 mm long with black snout, legs, and underside. Females drill into rose buds with their long snout and lay eggs inside; buds fail to open and may drop. Larvae develop inside the bud, then drop to soil to pupate. Also damages rose hips.

Cultivate soil under rose plantings in early spring to disrupt overwintering pupae.
entries.prevention: Inspect rose nursery stock before purchase. Maintain clean cultivation under rose beds through the season.

entries.symptoms: Adults 8 to 10 mm long with cream-colored wing covers marked by three brown longitudinal stripes (one of which terminates in an exclamation-point dot). Larvae are humpbacked, yellow-green with darker stripes. Both stages defoliate sunflower; severe early infestations skeletonize seedlings.
organicManagement: Handpick adults from small plantings; both adults and larvae are conspicuous and slow-moving.
Encourage natural enemies: predatory stink bugs and parasitic flies provide some suppression.
Tolerate moderate defoliation on plants past the V8 stage; sunflower compensates well.
entries.prevention: Crop rotation away from sunflower on a 2-year cycle. Manage weedy edges of fields where adults overwinter under crop debris.

entries.symptoms: Adults 5 mm long with metallic blue-black wing covers, orange-red thorax and legs. Larvae are slug-like, covered with fecal slime; both stages feed on cereal leaves leaving narrow longitudinal feeding scars between leaf veins, producing a 'window-paned' appearance.
organicManagement: Conserve parasitic wasp Tetrastichus julis, the established biocontrol agent; avoid spring insecticide use.
Spinosad sprays at early larval stage in heavy outbreaks (organic-approved formulations).
Light infestations rarely warrant treatment; cereals compensate well unless flag leaf is damaged.
entries.prevention: Avoid planting spring grains adjacent to fall-planted small grains. Manage volunteer cereal grain weeds in adjacent fields.

entries.symptoms: Small (3 to 4 mm) gray-brown weevil with long thread-like antennae and short snout. Notches leaf margins of strawberry, herbaceous perennials, and ornamental shrubs at night. Larvae feed on roots in soil. Adults play dead and drop when disturbed.
organicManagement: Beneficial nematodes (Heterorhabditis bacteriophora) for larval suppression in heavily infested strawberry beds.
Diatomaceous earth dusting at strawberry crown level disrupts adult feeding.
Rotate strawberry plantings to fresh ground on a 3-year cycle.
entries.prevention: Inspect strawberry transplants for hitchhiking adults. Avoid siting new strawberry beds adjacent to weedy field margins.

entries.symptoms: Small (2 to 3.5 mm) reddish-brown weevil with two black spots and a pale band on wing covers. Lays eggs on pods in the field and on dry beans in storage; larvae bore into seeds and consume them from inside. Emergence holes (1 to 2 mm round) and characteristic frass indicate infestation.
organicManagement: Freeze stored beans at minus 18 C for 4 days before storage; kills all life stages.
Store dry beans in sealed glass jars or food-grade buckets with tight-fitting lids.
Bay leaves or food-grade diatomaceous earth in storage containers as desiccant repellent.
entries.prevention: Harvest beans before pod drying allows weevil entry. Cure and dry beans rapidly in low humidity. Inspect stored beans monthly and freeze again at first sign of emergence.

entries.symptoms: Large green caterpillars 80 to 100 mm at maturity, with seven white diagonal stripes (vs five on tomato hornworm) and a red curved horn (vs black on tomato hornworm). Defoliate solanaceous crops from the top down. Sister species of tomato hornworm; often co-occurs.
organicManagement: Handpick caterpillars into soapy water; the most effective method for small gardens.
Spray Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis var. kurstaki) on foliage; works on larvae under 40 mm.
Conserve parasitic braconid wasps; leave wasp-parasitized caterpillars (white rice-like cocoons on the back) in place to release the next wasp generation.
Companion plant with basil, dill, marigold to attract braconid wasps.
entries.prevention: Fall tilling exposes overwintering pupae to predators and freezing. Rotate solanaceous crops on a 3-year cycle. Inspect plants twice weekly from July through August.

entries.symptoms: Pale green to brown caterpillars 25 to 30 mm at maturity with a distinct dark spot on each side just above the second true leg. Feed gregariously when young, dispersing as they mature. Skeletonize leaves and bore into fruit and flower clusters. Highly polyphagous; major on lettuce, tomato, pepper, asparagus, and many flowers.
organicManagement: Spinosad (organic-approved formulations) is effective on small larvae; respect re-entry intervals.
Bt subsp. kurstaki on early instars; less effective on larvae over 25 mm.
Conserve parasitic wasps Hyposoter exiguae and Chelonus insularis; common in unsprayed gardens.
Handpick mature larvae from high-value plantings.
entries.prevention: Avoid late-summer succession plantings of lettuce in southern regions when armyworm pressure peaks. Manage weedy field edges that harbor early-season populations.

entries.symptoms: Dark caterpillars up to 45 mm at maturity with two prominent yellow stripes along the back and a series of triangular black markings on each side. Polyphagous on vegetables, field crops, and ornamentals. Often migrates in 'army' fashion from harvested fields to neighboring crops.
organicManagement: Bt subsp. kurstaki on early instars; spinosad on later instars.
Encourage parasitic flies (Tachinidae) and wasps (Trichogramma) with diverse flowering field edges.
Handpick mature larvae visible at night by flashlight.
entries.prevention: Manage weeds in field margins to reduce early-season host plants. Avoid leaving harvested fields fallow with crop residue during armyworm flight peaks.

entries.symptoms: Smooth caterpillars up to 35 mm at maturity, dark gray-brown with a wide pale subdorsal stripe and a triangular dark mark on the first abdominal segment. Highly polyphagous in tropical and subtropical regions; reaches damaging populations on amaranth, pigweed, and cultivated crops in the SE US.
organicManagement: Bt subsp. kurstaki on early instars.
Spinosad for larger larvae and heavier outbreaks.
Encourage Cotesia parasitoids with sweet alyssum borders.
entries.prevention: Manage pigweed and amaranth weed reservoirs in adjacent fields. Avoid late-season succession plantings in heavily affected southern regions.

entries.symptoms: Smooth caterpillars up to 35 mm at maturity, dark gray-brown with a wide pale subdorsal stripe and a triangular dark mark on the first abdominal segment. entries.symptoms: Caterpillars 30 to 35 mm at maturity, color varies from pale green to reddish brown, with three or four longitudinal pale stripes. Bore into flower buds and immature fruit of geranium, petunia, tomato, and pepper. Sister species of corn earworm (Helicoverpa zea); distinguished by less prominent transverse bands and finer setae.
organicManagement: Bt subsp. kurstaki targeting larvae before they enter buds and fruit.
Spinosad spray on early instars at first sign of damage.
Remove and destroy infested buds and fruit; do not compost.
entries.prevention: Avoid planting tobacco near vegetable gardens; tobacco is a strong source of budworm migration. Manage geranium and petunia plantings as scouting sentinels for first arrival.

entries.symptoms: Pale green caterpillars 30 to 40 mm at maturity that move in a looping fashion (two pairs of abdominal prolegs missing, similar to cabbage looper). Has two thin white stripes along the back. Feeds on soybean and other legumes from below the upper canopy, creating shot-hole damage that progresses to skeletonization.
organicManagement: Bt subsp. kurstaki on early instars; coverage of leaf undersides is critical.
Encourage parasitic wasps (Copidosoma floridanum, Microplitis demolitor) by leaving flowering field margins.
Spinosad as a last resort on heavy outbreaks.
entries.prevention: Plant earlier-maturing soybean varieties in southern regions to escape peak looper flight in August. Conserve natural enemies by avoiding broad-spectrum insecticides.

entries.symptoms: Green to brown caterpillars 35 to 45 mm at maturity with three thin yellow stripes along each side and one yellow stripe down the back. Highly active when disturbed (wiggle violently). Major defoliator of soybean and other legumes in SE US; outbreaks can completely defoliate fields in 7 to 10 days.
organicManagement: Bt subsp. kurstaki is highly effective on this species; treat at first sign of small larvae.
Nuclear polyhedrosis virus (Anticarsia gemmatalis NPV) is commercially available in some regions; species-specific biocontrol.
Conserve generalist predators (stink bugs, spiders, ground beetles).
entries.prevention: Scout soybean weekly from R1 through R6 reproductive stages. Plant trap crop of velvetbean (Mucuna pruriens) where appropriate; concentrates pressure.

entries.symptoms: Young caterpillars are yellow with rows of black spots; mature larvae (25 mm) lose the spots and become green to copper-colored. Bores into cucurbit flowers and fruit, leaving frass at entry holes. Damage especially severe on summer squash and cucumber; melons less affected by feeding but secondary rot is severe.
organicManagement: Spinosad sprays at first flower; reapply every 5 to 7 days through bloom.
Floating row cover until first female flowers open; remove for pollination.
Plant trap crop of summer squash 14 days ahead of main planting to concentrate pressure; remove and destroy infested trap-crop fruit.
entries.prevention: Avoid late-summer cucurbit plantings in southern regions when pickleworm pressure peaks. Inspect fruit daily and remove anything with entry holes immediately.

entries.symptoms: Pale green caterpillars up to 30 mm at maturity with two thin white stripes along the back. Primarily a foliage feeder (unlike pickleworm) but will bore into fruit when foliage is gone. Causes characteristic shot-hole feeding patterns on cucurbit leaves.
organicManagement: Bt subsp. kurstaki and spinosad both effective on early instars.
Floating row cover until first female flowers open.
Encourage parasitic wasps with flowering insectary borders.
entries.prevention: Crop rotation between cucurbit and non-cucurbit plantings. Avoid late-summer plantings in heavily affected southern regions.

entries.symptoms: Pale green caterpillars up to 30 mm at maturity with two thin white stripes along the back. Primarily a foliage feeder (unlike pickleworm) but will bore into fruit when foliage is gone. Causes characteristic shot-hole feeding patterns on cucurbit leaves.

entries.symptoms: Pale green caterpillars up to 35 mm at maturity with three white stripes along the back. Feed on developing apple and pear fruit from petal fall through June, gouging deep holes that heal over into corky scars at harvest. Distinct from codling moth (which enters fruit later, leaves smaller wounds, fills with frass).
organicManagement: Bt subsp. kurstaki at petal fall and first cover; specifically targets early-season caterpillars.
Pheromone traps (Lithophane lures) to detect first flight; treat 7 to 10 days after first sustained catch.
Encourage parasitoid wasps; common natural enemies in unsprayed orchards.
entries.prevention: Maintain Bt cover from petal fall through 4 weeks post petal fall in cool spring regions where green fruitworm is established. Sanitize windfall promptly.

entries.prevention: Maintain Bt cover from petal fall through 4 weeks post petal fall in cool spring regions where green fruitworm is established. Sanitize windfall promptly.

entries.symptoms: Caterpillars 25 to 30 mm at maturity, color varies from pale yellow to dark gray with long white hairs and a dark stripe down the back. Build large messy gauzy webs at the tips of branches, enclosing the leaves they consume. Easy to spot from a distance. Two generations per year in most regions.
organicManagement: Prune off web-enclosed branch tips and destroy; the most effective treatment for residential settings.
Bt subsp. kurstaki sprayed directly onto webs after opening them with a long stick.
Encourage native parasitoid wasps and birds; do not treat with broad-spectrum insecticides that disrupt biocontrol.
Tolerate light infestations on mature trees; damage is mostly cosmetic and trees compensate well.
entries.prevention: Inspect fruit trees and ornamental shrubs in late June and early August (the two generations) for early-stage webs.

entries.symptoms: Black caterpillars 50 mm at maturity with a continuous white stripe down the back, blue spots on the sides, and long brown hairs. Build a single thick silken tent in the crotch of a tree where branches meet the trunk (distinct from fall webworm, which builds tents at branch tips). Emerge in spring; complete one generation by early summer.
organicManagement: Prune out tents in the early morning when caterpillars are inside; destroy by burning or submerging in soapy water.
Bt subsp. kurstaki sprayed directly into tents after opening.
Encourage birds and parasitoid wasps; tolerate light infestations on mature trees.
entries.prevention: Inspect host trees (apple, cherry, hawthorn) in fall for the dark shiny egg cases that ring small twigs; remove and destroy. Scout for tents at bud break.

entries.symptoms: Hairy caterpillars 50 mm at maturity with five pairs of blue dots followed by six pairs of red dots along the back. Major defoliator of oaks and many fruit and ornamental trees. Egg masses (light brown, fuzzy, 25 mm long) on tree trunks, walls, and outdoor furniture. Formerly known as 'gypsy moth'; renamed in 2022.
organicManagement: Scrape and destroy egg masses in fall and winter using a putty knife and soapy water container.
Bt subsp. kurstaki spray during early larval stage (typically May in temperate regions).
Burlap bands around tree trunks: caterpillars hide under during midday; check daily and destroy.
Nuclear polyhedrosis virus and the parasitic fungus Entomophaga maimaiga provide partial biocontrol in established invasion zones.
entries.prevention: Inspect outdoor furniture, vehicles, and firewood being moved between regions for egg masses. Comply with quarantine regulations.

entries.symptoms: Small (8 to 10 mm wingspan) brown moths whose larvae bore into grape clusters at multiple growth stages. Pre-bloom larvae web flower buds together; post-bloom larvae enter berries and feed inside, creating purple-spotted shriveled berries connected by silk. Secondary rot (Botrytis) usually follows.
organicManagement: Mating disruption with pheromone dispensers (Isomate GBM) in vineyards over 2 hectares; less effective in small plantings.
Bt subsp. kurstaki sprayed at pre-bloom and first generation egg hatch.
Spinosad rotation for resistance management.
Remove and destroy infested clusters during scouting; do not compost in vicinity of vineyard.
entries.prevention: Manage wild grapevines in adjacent woodlots, which serve as overwintering reservoirs. Scout pheromone traps from bloom through veraison.

entries.symptoms: Small (5 mm) cream-colored maggots that tunnel into germinating seeds and seedling roots of corn, beans, peas, cucurbits, and other large-seeded crops. Cause poor stands and stunted seedlings. Damage is hidden until seedlings fail to emerge or emerge weak; excavating gaps in the row reveals tunneled seed pieces.
organicManagement: Wait for soil temperatures to exceed 18 C before planting susceptible crops; rapid germination outpaces maggot damage.
Avoid heavy spring incorporation of fresh manure or green manure 2 to 3 weeks before planting; the resulting attractant volatiles draw egg-laying flies.
Floating row cover at planting in heavy-pressure fields.
entries.prevention: Plant into well-drained warm soil. Avoid heavy fall manure on fields intended for spring bean and corn plantings. Use treated seed only as a last resort if organic certification permits.

entries.symptoms: Adults look like large mosquitoes (25 mm long with very long thin legs) and are harmless. Larvae are gray-brown leatherjackets up to 30 mm long that feed on roots and crowns of turfgrass, vegetable seedlings, and ornamentals. Damage shows as dead patches in lawn and stunted, easily uprooted seedlings.
organicManagement: Beneficial nematodes (Steinernema feltiae) drenched into moist soil in late summer; targets young larvae.
Roll lawns in spring; mechanical disturbance reduces larval survival.
Encourage starlings and other ground-feeding birds, which are effective larval predators.
entries.prevention: Aerate compacted lawns and improve drainage; leatherjacket pressure correlates strongly with saturated soils. Avoid converting turf to vegetable beds without a fallow season in regions with established crane fly populations.
entries.source: Penn State Extension; PNW Insect Management Handbook

entries.symptoms: Tiny (1.5 mm) translucent yellow larvae feeding hidden inside growing points of brassica crops. Symptoms: distorted growing tips, twisted leaves, swollen petioles, blind heads on broccoli and cauliflower, no harvestable heads. Among the most damaging recent invasives on brassicas in northeastern North America.
organicManagement: Floating row cover from transplant through harvest is the single most effective tool; midges cannot pass.
Crop rotation away from brassicas for 2 to 3 years on infested fields.
Plow infested crop residue under deeply (15 cm minimum) to disrupt overwintering pupae.
entries.prevention: Use insect netting (under 1.0 mm mesh) over all brassica plantings in established regions. Avoid planting brassicas within 500 m of last year's infested field.

entries.symptoms: Adults are 6 to 8 mm tephritid flies with distinctive dark patterned wings and a yellow scutellum. Larvae are 8 mm yellow maggots that tunnel through walnut and peach husks, causing the husk to soften, blacken, and stain the nut shell. Late infestations reduce nut quality and storability; early infestations can cause complete loss.
organicManagement: Yellow sticky traps with ammonium carbonate lure to monitor adult emergence; treat 10 to 14 days after first sustained catch.
Spinosad bait sprays (GF-120) on the lower canopy at first egg-laying; effective and selective.
Sanitize windfall walnuts daily during emergence to disrupt larval pupation.
entries.prevention: Apply kaolin clay to husk surfaces beginning at first egg-laying. Pick up dropped walnuts twice weekly through harvest.

entries.symptoms: Tiny (1 mm) larvae feeding in growing tips and flower buds of blueberry. Causes shoot tip dieback, blackened buds that fail to open, and flower bud abortion. Especially damaging on southern highbush and rabbiteye types. Multiple generations per season.
organicManagement: Prune off blackened shoot tips during dormant pruning; destroy clippings rather than chipping in place.
Spinosad applied at first flower bud swell.
Encourage parasitic wasps (Aprostocetus, Synopeas); common but slow-acting.
entries.prevention: Scout fall flower bud development; midge feeding is visible as flagged shoots. Maintain plant vigor with appropriate fertility; stressed plants suffer disproportionately.

entries.symptoms: Small (5 mm) cream-colored larvae that tunnel inside the upper portion of new raspberry primocanes in spring. Symptoms: wilting of cane tips (the 'crook' or 'shepherd's crook'), eventual death of the affected cane terminal. Damage is unmistakable in spring; pruning to live tissue reveals tunnels and frass.
organicManagement: Prune wilted cane tips 5 cm below the lowest visible damage; destroy clippings rather than mulching in place.
Continue pruning weekly during the spring damage window (typically May in temperate regions).
Floating row cover briefly during egg-laying flight if pressure is severe.
entries.prevention: Sanitation: remove and destroy infested cane tips before larvae complete development. Do not allow damaged tips to remain in the planting.

entries.symptoms: Adults resemble small bumblebees (12 mm), variable in color (yellow, orange, brown bands), with single larva per bulb. Larvae are off-white maggots up to 18 mm that hollow out daffodil, narcissus, and amaryllis bulbs over the course of a season. Symptoms appear the following spring as failure to emerge or yellow stunted leaves.
organicManagement: Lift and inspect bulbs after foliage dieback; cull soft, hollow, or light-weight bulbs.
Hot water treatment of bulbs at 43 C for 3 hours before replanting kills larvae inside.
Cover daffodil plantings with floating row cover during late spring fly activity (typically May to June).
entries.prevention: Cultivate soil surface around bulb plantings after foliage dies back to disrupt entry sites. Avoid leaving harvested daffodils on the soil surface, which attract egg-laying adults.

entries.symptoms: Adults 17 mm long, shield-shaped, marbled brown above with alternating dark and light bands on the antennae and abdominal edge. Highly polyphagous: tree fruit, vegetables, ornamentals. Feeding causes deep dimples, brown depressions, and corky tissue on fruit and pods; on pepper and apple the damage is visible at harvest as 'cat-facing'. Major overwintering household nuisance.
organicManagement: Pyramid traps (commercial: Rescue, Trece) baited with aggregation pheromone effective for monitoring; some suppression in dense traplines.
Floating row cover on tomato, pepper, and bean from flowering through harvest.
Kaolin clay (Surround WP) at 50 lb per acre weekly on apple, pear, and peach during fruit development.
Vacuum bugs from foliage in small plantings, especially on overwintering aggregations on building exteriors.
entries.prevention: Seal building exterior cracks before fall. In orchards, manage perimeter (woodlot edges and tree-of-heaven) where bugs aggregate before invasion.

entries.symptoms: Adults 13 to 17 mm long, uniform bright green, shield-shaped, with three small white dots between the shoulders. Polyphagous on soybean, bean, tomato, pepper, and cotton. Feeding produces hard yellow callus tissue on bean pods, sunken brown spots on tomato fruit, and seed damage on soybean. Vectors yeast spot disease.
organicManagement: Trap crops of pigeon pea, sunflower, or sorghum planted around soybean fields concentrate adults; mow or destroy when populations build.
Floating row cover on vegetable beds during peak migration.
Encourage tachinid fly Trichopoda pennipes (introduced biocontrol); orange wasp-like flies are easily identified.
entries.prevention: Scout from R3 stage on soybean. Do not plant susceptible vegetables adjacent to overwintered soybean stubble in southern regions.

entries.symptoms: Adults 13 to 17 mm long, uniform bright green, shield-shaped, with three small white dots between the shoulders. Polyphagous on soybean, bean, tomato, pepper, and cotton. Feeding produces hard yellow callus tissue on bean pods, sunken brown spots on tomato fruit, and seed damage on soybean. Vectors yeast spot disease.
organicManagement: Trap crops of pigeon pea, sunflower, or sorghum planted around soybean fields concentrate adults; mow or destroy when populations build.
Floating row cover on vegetable beds during peak migration.
Encourage tachinid fly Trichopoda pennipes (introduced biocontrol); orange wasp-like flies are easily identified.
