entries.symptoms: Adults 12 to 15 mm, shield-shaped, mottled brown above with yellow underside. Native US polyphage; common on corn, soybean, cotton, and tree fruit. Feeding on developing corn kernels causes 'banana' kernel deformation; on apple causes corky depressions that grow with the fruit.
organicManagement: Kaolin clay protectant on tree fruit during early fruit development.
Trap cropping with sorghum-sudangrass around vegetable beds.
Maintain weedy field borders for natural enemies (wheel bug, big-eyed bug, predatory stink bug Podisus maculiventris).
entries.prevention: Scout corn and soybean at silking and pod fill. Manage host weed reservoirs (mullein, vetch).

entries.symptoms: Adults 13 to 19 mm, bright pure green, shield-shaped, larger and brighter than southern green stink bug. Native US polyphage on vegetables, soybean, and tree fruit. Damage similar to other Pentatomidae: corky depressions on fruit, seed damage on legumes, banana kernels on corn.
organicManagement: Same as brown stink bug: kaolin clay, trap cropping, natural enemy conservation.
Hand-removal on small plantings of tomato and pepper; bugs are conspicuous on green fruit.
Vacuum from foliage in dense infestations.
entries.prevention: Manage weed reservoirs of host plants (mullein, vetch, soybean stubble). Scout high-value vegetables from flowering onward.

entries.symptoms: Large (20 mm) elongate brown bugs with characteristic flat leaf-like expansions on the hind legs and a thin white band across the back. Feed on tomato, peach, citrus, pomegranate. Damage to fruit similar to stink bugs but feeding holes are usually larger (deep cone-shaped pits). Common in SE US.
organicManagement: Vacuum or knock bugs into soapy water from foliage; large enough to handle individually.
Floating row cover on tomato and pepper during peak migration.
Encourage tachinid flies (Trichopoda pennipes) and assassin bugs as natural enemies.
entries.prevention: Scout fall pomegranate and citrus closely; aggregations form on developing fruit. Manage weedy field borders.

entries.symptoms: Adults 18 to 20 mm, similar to L. phyllopus but with two distinct yellow spots on the upper back (the thorax) plus a white zigzag band across the wings. Major pest on almond (causes 'kernel spot' on developing nuts) and pomegranate. Has expanded north into central California vineyards in recent years.
organicManagement: Sanitation: remove and destroy mummified almonds and overwintered pomegranates that harbor adults.
Vacuum overwintering aggregations on building exteriors and tree trunks.
Kaolin clay protectant on almond during nut fill.
entries.prevention: Manage citrus and pomegranate trees in adjacent residential plantings, which serve as primary overwintering hosts.

entries.symptoms: Adults 18 to 20 mm, similar to L. phyllopus but with two distinct yellow spots on the upper back (the thorax) plus a white zigzag band across the wings. entries.symptoms: Adults 12 to 14 mm, elongate, black with bright red-orange margins and three longitudinal red stripes on the thorax. Nymphs are bright red with black legs. Massive overwintering aggregations on south-facing walls of houses are the most familiar nuisance; feeds primarily on boxelder, also on maple and ash. Does not bite, does not damage homes, but stains surfaces.
organicManagement: Vacuum aggregations from exterior walls; do not crush, as the bugs stain.
Soapy water spray (1% concentration) directly on aggregations; the soap penetrates the cuticle.
Tolerate on host trees; damage is cosmetic.
entries.prevention: Seal building exterior cracks before fall. Remove female boxelder trees within 200 m of homes if nuisance is severe; boxelder bug requires female (seeded) trees to complete its life cycle.

entries.symptoms: Adults 7 mm, bright yellow-green with four distinct black longitudinal stripes on the wings. Feeds on mint, basil, currant, gooseberry, and many herbaceous perennials. Damage shows as round dark sunken spots about 1 to 2 mm in diameter on upper leaf surfaces; spots may coalesce. Often mistaken for fungal disease.
organicManagement: Insecticidal soap during nymph stage; less effective on adults.
Handpick adults in early morning when sluggish.
Tolerate light damage on mint and basil; affected leaves can be trimmed and plant flushes new growth quickly.
entries.prevention: Remove and destroy crop residue and weedy edges in fall; eggs overwinter in plant stems. Disturb soil under perennial herb beds in early spring to destroy emerging nymphs.

entries.symptoms: Adults 5 to 6 mm, mottled brown-green with a pale Y-shaped mark on the scutellum. Feeds on flowers and developing fruit of strawberry, alfalfa, cotton, and many vegetables. On strawberry causes 'cat-facing': deformed, hardened fruit with hollowed receptacle. Major economic pest in western US.
organicManagement: Vacuum (D-Vac suction) from strawberry blocks; targets adults during morning feeding.
Maintain alfalfa strip-cropping as a trap; mow alfalfa before bug migration to strawberry.
Sweet alyssum borders attract Anaphes iole, the primary parasitoid.
entries.prevention: Avoid weedy strawberry blocks; many broadleaf weeds (lambsquarters, pigweed, mustards) host populations. Strip cropping with alfalfa is more effective than alfalfa removal.

entries.symptoms: Small (3 to 4 mm) wedge-shaped leafhoppers, olive-green to yellow with six diagnostic black spots on the head. Most economically important as the vector of aster yellows phytoplasma, which causes purple leaf reddening, witches-broom growth, deformed carrot tops, and 'green flower' on aster, marigold, and many ornamentals. Direct feeding damage is minor compared to disease transmission.
organicManagement: Floating row cover on carrot beds from emergence through canopy closure.
Insecticidal soap on heavy populations; coverage of leaf undersides is critical.
Manage perennial weeds (plantain, daisy fleabane, dandelion) that harbor both leafhoppers and aster yellows reservoirs.
entries.prevention: Plant carrot beds away from old hay fields and grain stubble. Yellow sticky cards monitor first arrival; treat or cover at first sustained catch.

entries.symptoms: Small (3 mm) pale yellow-green leafhoppers, wedge-shaped, with subtle dark markings on wings. The major vector of beet curly top virus (BCTV), which causes severe upward leaf curl, stunting, and crop failure on tomato, pepper, bean, and sugar beet. Even brief feeding by a viruliferous leafhopper produces BCTV. The leafhopper itself does not cause significant direct damage.
organicManagement: Floating row cover on tomato, pepper, and bean transplants from planting through bloom; main vector activity window.
Maintain landscape diversity around vegetable beds; monoculture rangelands favor curly top epidemics.
Source virus-tested transplants; do not save seed from BCTV-symptomatic plants.
entries.prevention: Avoid planting susceptible crops adjacent to dry rangeland or weedy lots, the leafhopper's overwintering reservoir. Plant resistant tomato cultivars where available.

entries.symptoms: Large (12 mm) leafhoppers, dark brown with mottled tan markings and translucent wings; legs and head have ivory yellow markings. Highly polyphagous xylem feeder; vectors the bacterium Xylella fastidiosa, causing Pierce's disease in grape, citrus variegated chlorosis, oleander leaf scorch, and almond leaf scorch. The vector is more important than direct feeding.
organicManagement: Yellow sticky cards (large size) to monitor arrival; treat at first sustained catch.
Kaolin clay sprays on susceptible vineyards as a feeding deterrent.
Conserve parasitic wasps (Gonatocerus ashmeadi, the imported biocontrol agent) by avoiding broad-spectrum insecticides.
entries.prevention: Manage adjacent citrus, oleander, and ornamental hosts. Comply with regional quarantine regulations on nursery stock movement.

entries.symptoms: Small (3 mm) leafhoppers, white to pale yellow with bright red markings on wings (or solid white in some populations). Feed on undersides of grape leaves, causing characteristic white stippling that progresses to leaf yellowing and premature defoliation. High populations reduce vine vigor and fruit quality.
organicManagement: Conserve native Anagrus parasitic wasps; maintain prune trees or wild grape near vineyards as alternate hosts for overwintering parasitoids.
Yellow sticky cards monitor populations; treat threshold is 5 to 10 nymphs per leaf.
Insecticidal soap on heavy populations during nymph stage; less effective on adults.
entries.prevention: Avoid stressing vines (water stress, excess nitrogen, herbicide drift) that increases leafhopper damage. Manage weeds within rows during early season.

entries.symptoms: Small (3 mm) leafhoppers, ivory white to pale yellow, on undersides of apple and pear leaves. Cause distinctive white stippling on upper leaf surfaces. Second-generation adults are a major harvest-time nuisance for pickers and pollute fruit with excreta. Two generations per year in most regions.
organicManagement: Conserve parasitic wasp Anagrus epos; well-established in unsprayed orchards.
Treat at first generation nymph stage (June) with insecticidal soap or summer oil; second-generation treatment is much less effective.
Avoid late-summer pyrethrin sprays that flare leafhopper populations and disrupt biocontrol.
entries.prevention: Maintain natural enemy populations through pesticide-free border zones. Plan harvest workforce protection (long sleeves, eyewear) during second-generation outbreaks.

entries.symptoms: Adults 25 mm long, gray forewings with black spots and pink-red hindwings with black spots and a black border. Massive aggregations on tree-of-heaven, grape, walnut, and many trees. Phloem feeders; cause vine and tree decline through copious honeydew, sooty mold, and direct sap loss. Invasive in NE US since 2014, spreading rapidly. Significant economic threat to grape.
organicManagement: Scrape and destroy egg masses (mud-like patches 25 to 40 mm on tree bark, walls, outdoor furniture) October through May.
Tree banding with sticky bands on tree-of-heaven traps nymphs; check for non-target captures (birds, beneficials).
Trap trees: leave a few tree-of-heaven and treat aggregations with insecticidal soap or kaolin clay.
Comply with quarantine regulations; do not move infested wood, vehicles, or outdoor goods.
entries.prevention: Manage tree-of-heaven (Ailanthus altissima) within 100 m of vineyards. Inspect vehicles, outdoor furniture, and firewood for egg masses before moving between regions.

Comply with quarantine regulations; do not move infested wood, vehicles, or outdoor goods.

entries.symptoms: Adults 25 mm long, gray forewings with black spots and pink-red hindwings with black spots and a black border. Massive aggregations on tree-of-heaven, grape, walnut, and many trees. Phloem feeders; cause vine and tree decline through copious honeydew, sooty mold, and direct sap loss. Invasive in NE US since 2014, spreading rapidly. Significant economic threat to grape.
organicManagement: Scrape and destroy egg masses (mud-like patches 25 to 40 mm on tree bark, walls, outdoor furniture) October through May.
Tree banding with sticky bands on tree-of-heaven traps nymphs; check for non-target captures (birds, beneficials).
Trap trees: leave a few tree-of-heaven and treat aggregations with insecticidal soap or kaolin clay.
Comply with quarantine regulations; do not move infested wood, vehicles, or outdoor goods.
entries.prevention: Manage tree-of-heaven (Ailanthus altissima) within 100 m of vineyards. Inspect vehicles, outdoor furniture, and firewood for egg masses before moving between regions.

entries.symptoms: Adult females are bright orange to red, 3 to 5 mm long, attached to bark or leaves with a conspicuous fluted white wax egg sac extending up to 12 mm behind. Found on citrus, oleander, pittosporum, and many ornamentals. Heavy infestations stunt and yellow plants; honeydew supports sooty mold. The famous biocontrol target of vedalia beetle (Rodolia cardinalis).
organicManagement: Conserve vedalia beetle (Rodolia cardinalis); this introduced predator controls cottony cushion scale in most years.
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that disrupt vedalia beetle populations and cause scale flares.
Spot treatment with horticultural oil at 2% on isolated heavy infestations.
entries.prevention: Maintain a chemical-free buffer in citrus around vedalia beetle reservoirs. Inspect new ornamental plants for white egg sacs before planting.

entries.symptoms: Adult females are bright orange to red, 3 to 5 mm long, attached to bark or leaves with a conspicuous fluted white wax egg sac extending up to 12 mm behind. Found on citrus, oleander, pittosporum, and many ornamentals. Heavy infestations stunt and yellow plants; honeydew supports sooty mold. The famous biocontrol target of vedalia beetle (Rodolia cardinalis).
organicManagement: Conserve vedalia beetle (Rodolia cardinalis); this introduced predator controls cottony cushion scale in most years.

entries.symptoms: Flat oval scales 3 to 4 mm long, light to dark brown, on twigs and leaves of citrus and many ornamentals. Soft scales (Coccidae) produce abundant honeydew, leading to sooty mold that blackens leaves and fruit. Most common indoor/greenhouse scale on houseplants.
organicManagement: Horticultural oil at 2% on dormant trees; coverage of all bark surfaces is critical.
Summer oil at 1% for crawler stage suppression; time with sticky tape monitoring for crawlers.
Conserve parasitic wasps Metaphycus luteolus and Coccophagus species; common in unsprayed plantings.
Remove ant colonies that tend scales for honeydew; ant exclusion alone often allows natural enemies to restore control.
entries.prevention: Maintain ant baits (boric acid stakes) around citrus and ornamentals. Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that flare scale populations.

entries.symptoms: Flat oval scales 3 to 4 mm long, light to dark brown, on twigs and leaves of citrus and many ornamentals. Soft scales (Coccidae) produce abundant honeydew, leading to sooty mold that blackens leaves and fruit. Most common indoor/greenhouse scale on houseplants.

organicManagement: Horticultural oil at 2% on dormant trees; coverage of all bark surfaces is critical.

entries.symptoms: Round armored scales 2 mm in diameter with a translucent red-brown cover, often with a darker central spot. Heavily infested fruit and twigs become covered with overlapping scales; fruit color stays green where scales attach. Heavy populations cause leaf yellowing, twig dieback, and reduce fruit quality.
organicManagement: Conserve parasitic wasps Aphytis melinus and Comperiella bifasciata; the cornerstone of California organic citrus management.
Release Aphytis melinus at 5,000 per acre in conventional-to-organic transitions for the first 2 seasons.
Spray narrow-range horticultural oil at 1.5% during crawler emergence (monitor with double-sided tape on twigs).
Manage Argentine ants, which protect scales from natural enemies.
entries.prevention: Avoid spring sulfur applications, which disrupt Aphytis populations. Maintain ant exclusion bands on tree trunks.

entries.symptoms: Round armored scales 2 mm in diameter with a translucent red-brown cover, often with a darker central spot. Heavily infested fruit and twigs become covered with overlapping scales; fruit color stays green where scales attach. Heavy populations cause leaf yellowing, twig dieback, and reduce fruit quality.
organicManagement: Conserve parasitic wasps Aphytis melinus and Comperiella bifasciata; the cornerstone of California organic citrus management.

entries.symptoms: Armored scales 3 mm long, brown, curved like a miniature oyster shell. Heavily infested apple twigs and branches develop a dark crust of overlapping scales; branches die back from the tips. Also damages lilac, ash, willow, dogwood, and many landscape trees. Two generations per year in southern regions, one in northern.
organicManagement: Dormant horticultural oil at 2% targets overwintering eggs under scale covers.
Pruning out heavily encrusted small branches and burning the prunings.
Conserve parasitic wasps (Aphytis mytilaspidis) and predatory mites; broad-spectrum insecticides cause scale outbreaks.
entries.prevention: Annual dormant oil on apple, lilac, and other susceptible plantings with prior history. Inspect nursery stock for scale crust before planting.

entries.symptoms: Armored scales 3 mm long, brown, curved like a miniature oyster shell. Heavily infested apple twigs and branches develop a dark crust of overlapping scales; branches die back from the tips. Also damages lilac, ash, willow, dogwood, and many landscape trees. Two generations per year in southern regions, one in northern.
organicManagement: Dormant horticultural oil at 2% targets overwintering eggs under scale covers.

entries.symptoms: Sexually dimorphic armored scales: females are dark brown, pear-shaped, 2 mm long; males are smaller, white, elongate. Heavy infestations produce a white-flecked appearance on lower stems and undersides of leaves. Major pest on Euonymus, pachysandra, English ivy, and bittersweet in eastern US ornamentals.
organicManagement: Dormant horticultural oil at 3% on deciduous Euonymus species.
Summer oil at 1% during crawler emergence (typically late May and again in late July).
Severe shearing of heavily infested ground covers (pachysandra) followed by oil application allows fresh growth to escape.
entries.prevention: Avoid planting Euonymus japonica and E. alata, the most susceptible species. Maintain plant vigor with appropriate fertility and water.

entries.symptoms: Soft white waxy insects 3 to 5 mm long with 18 short marginal wax filaments and a pair of slightly longer terminal filaments. Cluster in protected crevices on citrus, grape, ornamental shrubs, and many houseplants. Produce honeydew supporting sooty mold; cause leaf yellowing, premature fruit drop, and twig dieback under heavy pressure.
organicManagement: Conserve parasitic wasps Leptomastix dactylopii and Anagyrus pseudococci; release in establishment-deficient plantings.
Release mealybug destroyer beetle (Cryptolaemus montrouzieri) at 2,000 per acre.
Spot treatment with insecticidal soap or 70% isopropyl alcohol applied with a cotton swab on individual colonies.
entries.prevention: Manage ant colonies that tend mealybugs and protect them from natural enemies. Inspect new plant introductions for white wax clusters.

entries.symptoms: Soft white waxy insects 3 to 5 mm long with 18 short marginal wax filaments and a pair of slightly longer terminal filaments. Cluster in protected crevices on citrus, grape, ornamental shrubs, and many houseplants. Produce honeydew supporting sooty mold; cause leaf yellowing, premature fruit drop, and twig dieback under heavy pressure.
organicManagement: Conserve parasitic wasps Leptomastix dactylopii and Anagyrus pseudococci; release in establishment-deficient plantings.
Release mealybug destroyer beetle (Cryptolaemus montrouzieri) at 2,000 per acre.
Spot treatment with insecticidal soap or 70% isopropyl alcohol applied with a cotton swab on individual colonies.
entries.prevention: Manage ant colonies that tend mealybugs and protect them from natural enemies. Inspect new plant introductions for white wax clusters.

entries.symptoms: Soft white waxy mealybugs similar in appearance to citrus mealybug but typically smaller and with a yellow-orange body color under the wax. Hidden under loose bark, in spurs, and in crown areas of grapevines. Vectors grapevine leafroll-associated viruses (GLRaV-1, GLRaV-3), causing irreversible vineyard decline.
organicManagement: Release Anagyrus pseudococci and Leptomastix dactylopii at 50,000 to 100,000 per acre in early season.
Mating disruption (vine mealybug pheromone dispensers) deployed at 1 acre minimum block size.
Insecticidal soap and horticultural oil during early-season exposed crawler period.
entries.prevention: Inspect grape nursery stock for crawlers before planting. Sanitize equipment between vineyard blocks.

entries.symptoms: Distinguished by two very long posterior wax filaments (the 'tails') that can equal or exceed body length. Common indoor mealybug on tropical houseplants, also major pest of citrus, avocado, and ornamentals outdoors in mild climates.
organicManagement: Spot treatment with 70% isopropyl alcohol on a cotton swab for individual colonies on houseplants.
Release mealybug destroyer beetle (Cryptolaemus montrouzieri) on large indoor plantings or in greenhouses.
Horticultural oil at 1% on tolerant outdoor crops.
entries.prevention: Quarantine new houseplant introductions for 2 weeks before adding to collection. Inspect tropical fruit (mango, avocado) at purchase.

entries.symptoms: Distinguished by two very long posterior wax filaments (the 'tails') that can equal or exceed body length. Common indoor mealybug on tropical houseplants, also major pest of citrus, avocado, and ornamentals outdoors in mild climates.
organicManagement: Spot treatment with 70% isopropyl alcohol on a cotton swab for individual colonies on houseplants.
Release mealybug destroyer beetle (Cryptolaemus montrouzieri) on large indoor plantings or in greenhouses.
Horticultural oil at 1% on tolerant outdoor crops.
entries.prevention: Quarantine new houseplant introductions for 2 weeks before adding to collection. Inspect tropical fruit (mango, avocado) at purchase.

entries.symptoms: Small (1 to 1.5 mm) thrips with brown to dark brown body. Major early-season pest on peanut, soybean, and cotton in SE US; also colonizes tomato and pepper. Vectors tomato spotted wilt virus (TSWV). Direct feeding causes characteristic silvery scarring on leaves; virus transmission is the larger threat.
organicManagement: Conserve minute pirate bug (Orius insidiosus), the dominant thrips predator in vegetable systems.
Reflective silver plastic mulch repels colonizing winged adults and delays TSWV onset.
Spinosad foliar sprays on heavy populations; rotate with neem to manage resistance.
entries.prevention: Plant tomato and pepper resistant cultivars where TSWV is established. Scout fields weekly from transplant through fruit set with yellow sticky cards.

entries.symptoms: Small (1 to 1.5 mm) thrips with brown to dark brown body. Major early-season pest on peanut, soybean, and cotton in SE US; also colonizes tomato and pepper. Vectors tomato spotted wilt virus (TSWV). Direct feeding causes characteristic silvery scarring on leaves; virus transmission is the larger threat.

entries.symptoms: Small (1 mm) pale yellow thrips, transparent in the immature stages. Major polyphagous pest in tropical and subtropical regions; expanding north in protected culture. Feeding causes severe silvering and bronzing of leaves; vectors several tospoviruses. Damage on cucurbit fruit is cosmetic but extensive.
organicManagement: Release predatory mites (Neoseiulus cucumeris or Amblyseius swirskii) at 50 per square meter weekly in greenhouses.
Yellow or blue sticky cards monitor populations; treat at 5 thrips per card per week.
Spinosad spray for outdoor outbreaks; respect re-entry interval and rotation with other modes of action.
entries.prevention: Insect netting on protected culture (mesh under 0.6 mm). Inspect transplants and tropical plant imports for early-stage thrips.

entries.symptoms: Small (1 mm) pale yellow thrips, transparent in the immature stages. Major polyphagous pest in tropical and subtropical regions; expanding north in protected culture. Feeding causes severe silvering and bronzing of leaves; vectors several tospoviruses. Damage on cucurbit fruit is cosmetic but extensive.
organicManagement: Release predatory mites (Neoseiulus cucumeris or Amblyseius swirskii) at 50 per square meter weekly in greenhouses.

entries.symptoms: Adults 1.5 mm long, dark brown to black with paler legs and reticulated body texture. Nymphs are pale yellow. Feeds on leaves of avocado, citrus, and many ornamentals causing silvery-gray stippling, leaf bronzing, and characteristic clusters of dark fecal pellets on leaf undersides.
organicManagement: Conserve predatory mites and minute pirate bugs in protected culture.
Release parasitoid Thripobius semiluteus (commercially available) for avocado.
Insecticidal soap and spinosad rotation for greenhouse infestations.
entries.prevention: Manage greenhouse weeds and adjacent ornamentals that harbor populations. Maintain greenhouse humidity above 50%; greenhouse thrips dislike high humidity.

entries.symptoms: Adults 1.5 mm long, dark brown to black with paler legs and reticulated body texture. Nymphs are pale yellow. Feeds on leaves of avocado, citrus, and many ornamentals causing silvery-gray stippling, leaf bronzing, and characteristic clusters of dark fecal pellets on leaf undersides.
organicManagement: Conserve predatory mites and minute pirate bugs in protected culture.
Release parasitoid Thripobius semiluteus (commercially available) for avocado.

entries.prevention: Manage greenhouse weeds and adjacent ornamentals that harbor populations. Maintain greenhouse humidity above 50%; greenhouse thrips dislike high humidity.

entries.symptoms: Adults 1.5 mm, dark brown. Both adults and nymphs feed on gladiolus and iris foliage and developing flower buds. Damage shows as silvery streaks on leaves, deformed flowers, and characteristic white-flecked petals on opening blooms. Heavy infestations cause buds to fail to open.
organicManagement: Hot water treatment of gladiolus corms at 49 C for 30 minutes before planting kills thrips in storage.
Spinosad sprays at first sign of damage on growing plants.
Yellow sticky cards monitor populations; treat at first sustained catch.
entries.prevention: Inspect stored corms for thrips before planting; dust with diatomaceous earth in storage. Rotate gladiolus plantings to fresh ground annually.

entries.symptoms: Adults 1.5 mm with white waxy wings, hold wings flat over the body. Nymphs are oval translucent scales on leaf undersides. Major pest of greenhouse and high-tunnel tomato, pepper, eggplant, and cucumber. Honeydew and sooty mold reduce fruit quality; heavy populations stunt plants.
organicManagement: Release parasitic wasp Encarsia formosa at 3 wasps per square meter weekly; the cornerstone of greenhouse whitefly biocontrol.
Yellow sticky cards monitor adult populations and capture migrating whiteflies.
Insecticidal soap on heavy populations; coverage of leaf undersides is critical.
Beauveria bassiana fungal biopesticide for indoor and tunnel applications.
entries.prevention: Insect screening on greenhouse vents (under 0.4 mm mesh). Maintain a 3-week host-free period between greenhouse crop cycles.

entries.symptoms: Adults 1 to 1.5 mm with white wings marked by a distinctive zigzag gray band. Common on cotton, tomato, sweet potato, and many vegetables in southern US. Major economic pest of cotton; on vegetables typically secondary to silverleaf whitefly but transmits sweet potato chlorotic stunt virus.
organicManagement: Same as silverleaf whitefly: encarsia parasitoids, reflective mulch, insecticidal soap, neem.
Reflective silver plastic mulch under transplants delays colonization.
Yellow sticky cards monitor populations; treat at 5 to 10 adults per card per week.
entries.prevention: Manage cotton and weed reservoirs (lambsquarters, velvetleaf) near vegetable beds. Plant susceptible vegetables on the upwind side of cotton fields.

entries.symptoms: Adults 1 to 1.5 mm with white wings marked by a distinctive zigzag gray band. Common on cotton, tomato, sweet potato, and many vegetables in southern US. Major economic pest of cotton; on vegetables typically secondary to silverleaf whitefly but transmits sweet potato chlorotic stunt virus.
organicManagement: Same as silverleaf whitefly: encarsia parasitoids, reflective mulch, insecticidal soap, neem.
Reflective silver plastic mulch under transplants delays colonization.
Yellow sticky cards monitor populations; treat at 5 to 10 adults per card per week.
entries.prevention: Manage cotton and weed reservoirs (lambsquarters, velvetleaf) near vegetable beds. Plant susceptible vegetables on the upwind side of cotton fields.

entries.symptoms: Brown spiral shells 25 to 35 mm in diameter, body brown with darker mottling. Feeds at night and during overcast wet weather on tender vegetables (lettuce, brassica seedlings), strawberry fruit, and citrus bark. Distinctive slime trails on leaves and soil. Major pest in California citrus and home gardens throughout the south-central US.
organicManagement: Iron phosphate bait (Sluggo, OMRI-approved) broadcast at 0.5 lb per 1000 sq ft; safe around pets and wildlife.
Copper foil or copper tape around raised beds; snails will not cross copper.
Trap with beer-baited containers or with inverted melon rinds; collect and destroy snails daily.
Release decollate snails (Rumina decollata) where permitted; specialist predator of brown garden snail.
entries.prevention: Eliminate daytime hiding places: stone walls, ground-cover mats, and dense low foliage. Water in the morning so soil surface dries by evening.

entries.symptoms: Brown spiral shells 25 to 35 mm in diameter, body brown with darker mottling. Feeds at night and during overcast wet weather on tender vegetables (lettuce, brassica seedlings), strawberry fruit, and citrus bark. Distinctive slime trails on leaves and soil. entries.symptoms: Mottled gray to brown slug 30 to 50 mm long, the most common garden slug worldwide. Feeds at night and during wet overcast weather on virtually every vegetable, ornamental, and bulb. Distinctive ragged holes in leaves with smooth edges and silver slime trails diagnostic. Severe seedling damage causes stand failures.
organicManagement: Iron phosphate bait (Sluggo, OMRI-approved); apply at planting and after every significant rain.
Beer traps (shallow containers buried to the rim) effective in small gardens.
Copper barrier strips around raised beds and individual plants.
Release predatory ground beetles (Carabidae) by maintaining straw mulch and undisturbed bed margins.
entries.prevention: Eliminate daytime hiding places: boards, stones, debris on bed surfaces. Reduce surface mulch in seedling beds. Avoid evening irrigation.
entries.source: UC IPM Online; Cornell Cooperative Extension

entries.symptoms: Gray oval crustaceans 10 to 15 mm long, flatter than pillbugs and unable to roll into a tight ball. Feed mostly on decaying organic matter; become pests on seedling vegetables (lettuce, brassicas), low-growing strawberry fruit, and damaged tissue. Sowbugs require moisture and cannot tolerate dry conditions.
organicManagement: Reduce surface mulch around seedlings until plants are established.
Iron phosphate bait (Sluggo Plus, contains spinosad against sowbugs) at planting.
Trap with rolled-up wet newspaper or cardboard placed on soil surface; collect and destroy daily.
Lift strawberries off soil with straw mulch or strawberry mats to reduce fruit contact.
entries.prevention: Improve drainage in raised beds. Cure compost fully before applying around seedlings. Eliminate daytime hiding sites: boards, stones, plant labels lying on soil.

entries.symptoms: Gray oval crustaceans 10 to 15 mm long, flatter than pillbugs and unable to roll into a tight ball. Feed mostly on decaying organic matter; become pests on seedling vegetables (lettuce, brassicas), low-growing strawberry fruit, and damaged tissue. Sowbugs require moisture and cannot tolerate dry conditions.
organicManagement: Reduce surface mulch around seedlings until plants are established.
Iron phosphate bait (Sluggo Plus, contains spinosad against sowbugs) at planting.
Trap with rolled-up wet newspaper or cardboard placed on soil surface; collect and destroy daily.
Lift strawberries off soil with straw mulch or strawberry mats to reduce fruit contact.
entries.prevention: Improve drainage in raised beds. Cure compost fully before applying around seedlings. Eliminate daytime hiding sites: boards, stones, plant labels lying on soil.

entries.symptoms: Gray oval crustaceans 10 to 15 mm long, flatter than pillbugs and unable to roll into a tight ball. Feed mostly on decaying organic matter; become pests on seedling vegetables (lettuce, brassicas), low-growing strawberry fruit, and damaged tissue. Sowbugs require moisture and cannot tolerate dry conditions.
organicManagement: Reduce surface mulch around seedlings until plants are established.
Iron phosphate bait (Sluggo Plus, contains spinosad against sowbugs) at planting.
Trap with rolled-up wet newspaper or cardboard placed on soil surface; collect and destroy daily.
Lift strawberries off soil with straw mulch or strawberry mats to reduce fruit contact.
entries.prevention: Improve drainage in raised beds. Cure compost fully before applying around seedlings. Eliminate daytime hiding sites: boards, stones, plant labels lying on soil.

entries.symptoms: Gray oval crustaceans 8 to 12 mm long that roll into a tight ball when disturbed (the 'roly-poly'). Similar habits and damage to sowbugs but more drought-tolerant. Feed primarily on decaying organic matter; pest on seedlings and on strawberry, melon, and squash fruit in direct soil contact.
organicManagement: Same approach as sowbug: reduce mulch around seedlings, trap with wet newspaper, lift fruit off soil.
Iron phosphate bait (Sluggo Plus) at planting and after rain.
Diatomaceous earth around seedling stems as a physical barrier.
entries.prevention: Same as sowbug: improve drainage, cure compost, eliminate hiding sites. Most damage occurs when populations are very high and other food is limited; reduce surface organic debris where pillbugs are abundant.

entries.symptoms: Gray oval crustaceans 8 to 12 mm long that roll into a tight ball when disturbed (the 'roly-poly'). Similar habits and damage to sowbugs but more drought-tolerant. entries.symptoms: Adults 12 to 15 mm long with conspicuous rear pincers (cerci). Dark brown body with reddish-brown wing covers. Nocturnal feeders on tender vegetable foliage, lettuce, dahlia and other flowers, sweet corn silks, and soft fruit (strawberry, peach). Beneficial against aphids and small caterpillars; net pest status depends on garden context.
organicManagement: Trap with rolled-up wet newspaper or 30 cm sections of garden hose placed in beds; collect and destroy earwigs in the morning.
Oil traps: bury tuna cans flush with soil and fill with 5 mm of vegetable oil; earwigs drown.
Diatomaceous earth around base of vulnerable plants.
Tolerate moderate populations as aphid biocontrol; act only when crop damage is significant.
entries.prevention: Eliminate daytime hiding places: rolled-up plant material, stones, lumber, dense ground covers. Reduce surface mulch immediately around vulnerable crops.

entries.symptoms: Light yellow-brown crickets 16 to 21 mm long, common around buildings, greenhouses, and outdoor lighting. Chew ragged holes in tender vegetable seedlings, lettuce, and dahlia foliage. Damage similar to grasshopper but mostly nocturnal. Distinctive chirping males in summer evenings.
organicManagement: Diatomaceous earth around seedling beds and along greenhouse perimeters.
Sticky traps along greenhouse walls and under benches.
Beneficial nematodes (Steinernema carpocapsae) in moist soil around bed perimeters.
entries.prevention: Reduce outdoor lighting near garden beds, which attracts adult crickets. Seal greenhouse entry points and weep holes.

entries.symptoms: Light yellow-brown crickets 16 to 21 mm long, common around buildings, greenhouses, and outdoor lighting. Chew ragged holes in tender vegetable seedlings, lettuce, and dahlia foliage. Damage similar to grasshopper but mostly nocturnal. Distinctive chirping males in summer evenings.
organicManagement: Diatomaceous earth around seedling beds and along greenhouse perimeters.

entries.symptoms: Stout brown crickets 25 to 35 mm long with massive shovel-like front legs adapted for digging. Tunnel through turfgrass, vegetable seedbeds, and sweet potato fields, raising surface tunnels and uprooting seedlings. Major economic pest in SE US turfgrass and pasture; significant home garden pest.
organicManagement: Beneficial nematodes (Steinernema scapterisci) specifically targets mole crickets; apply in spring to moist soil at dusk.
Spinosad bait broadcast in late summer when nymphs are small.
Soap flush test (2 tablespoons dish soap in 2 gallons water poured on a 1 sq m area) drives crickets to surface for inspection.
entries.prevention: Reduce irrigation duration; mole crickets favor consistently moist soil. Maintain healthy dense turf, which resists damage better than thin stands.

entries.symptoms: Large (40 to 50 mm) grasshoppers, body yellow-green to brown with distinctive black herringbone markings on the hind femurs. One of the most destructive North American grasshoppers; defoliates vegetables, alfalfa, corn, soybean, and ornamentals. Outbreak years follow dry summers with mild winters.
organicManagement: Nosema locustae bait (Semaspore, OMRI-approved) broadcast at 1 lb per acre when grasshoppers are at 3rd to 4th instar; targets young hoppers.
Floating row cover on high-value vegetables during outbreak years.
Encourage birds and beneficial insects; tolerate populations during non-outbreak years.
entries.prevention: Manage rangeland and ditch-bank grasshopper egg pods through grazing or mowing in fall. Tilling fall vegetable beds destroys overwintering egg pods.

entries.symptoms: Large (35 to 45 mm) grasshoppers with two prominent pale yellow stripes running the full length of the body from head to wing tips. Common in northern US and Canada; major pest in mixed grain-vegetable farming areas. Same damage profile as differential grasshopper.
organicManagement: Same as differential grasshopper: Nosema locustae bait, row cover, fall tillage.
Maintain fence-line grass strips short to reduce egg-pod habitat.
entries.prevention: Fall tillage of vegetable beds. Monitor grasshopper egg counts in fall to anticipate outbreak years.

entries.symptoms: Large (35 to 45 mm) grasshoppers with two prominent pale yellow stripes running the full length of the body from head to wing tips. Common in northern US and Canada; major pest in mixed grain-vegetable farming areas. Same damage profile as differential grasshopper.

entries.symptoms: Massive (60 to 80 mm) brightly-colored grasshoppers, juveniles black with yellow and red markings, adults dull yellow with red and black. Slow, clumsy, day-active. Highly toxic if eaten (cantharidin and similar compounds); secretes a foamy froth when disturbed. Significant pest of citrus, ornamentals, and vegetables in SE US.
organicManagement: Handpick juveniles in spring while they aggregate in roadside ditches; this is the most effective stage to control.
Adults are difficult to control once dispersed; nymphal stage management is critical.
Wear gloves and avoid skin contact with secretions during handling.
entries.prevention: Inspect early spring for aggregating black-and-yellow nymphs in ditches and field margins; treat immediately while still concentrated.

entries.symptoms: Adults 25 to 30 mm with black body, red eyes, and orange-veined wings. Emerge in massive synchronized broods (Brood X, Brood XIV, etc.) every 13 or 17 years depending on species and region. Females oviposit in pencil-thick twigs of fruit trees and ornamentals, causing 'flagging' (terminal dieback) where the twig splits at egg slits.
organicManagement: Cover small fruit trees (under 4 m) with fine mesh netting during the 4 to 6 week emergence window.
Delay planting young fruit trees in the season before a known emergence year.
Prune out flagged twigs after emergence ends; do not let split twigs remain on the tree.
Tolerate adults on mature trees; cicada damage is rarely lethal to established trees.
entries.prevention: Plan fruit-tree plantings around known brood emergence cycles. Cherry, apple, peach, plum, and oak suffer the most twig damage during emergence years.

entries.symptoms: Adults 30 mm with green and black body and clear veined wings. Annual cicada (not periodical), present every summer in late July through September. Loud daytime chorus. Female oviposition causes minor twig damage similar to periodical cicada but at much lower density; rarely economically important.
organicManagement: No treatment generally needed; tolerate damage to mature trees.
Cover young fruit trees if local populations are unusually high for a single season.
entries.prevention: Plant young fruit trees in late summer or fall to avoid the egg-laying window. Tolerance is the appropriate strategy for established trees.

entries.symptoms: Adults 30 mm with green and black body and clear veined wings. Annual cicada (not periodical), present every summer in late July through September. Loud daytime chorus. Female oviposition causes minor twig damage similar to periodical cicada but at much lower density; rarely economically important.
organicManagement: No treatment generally needed; tolerate damage to mature trees.

entries.symptoms: Adults 6 to 7 mm with gray-and-black thorax marked by four narrow black stripes and red-brown eyes. Larvae (maggots) are cream-colored and develop in decaying organic matter, manure, and compost. Not a crop pest directly but mechanically vectors enteric pathogens and is a nuisance around outdoor food gardens, livestock, and compost.
organicManagement: Manage breeding habitat: turn compost regularly, cover manure piles, eliminate standing wet organic matter.
Sticky fly tapes or jar traps with apple cider vinegar around garden areas.
Encourage parasitoid wasps (Spalangia, Muscidifurax) by leaving an undisturbed compost zone.
Beauveria bassiana biopesticide for indoor and greenhouse infestations.
entries.prevention: Maintain compost C:N ratio to support thermophilic breakdown rather than maggot breeding. Cover food and harvest containers in the garden.

entries.symptoms: Adult wasps 15 to 20 mm with slender waist, yellow-and-brown or yellow-and-black markings; long dangling legs in flight. Build open paper combs in protected locations (eaves, sheds, dense shrubs). Important predators of caterpillars, sawfly larvae, and small soft-bodied insects. Become pests on ripe orchard fruit (especially apple, pear, grape) and around outdoor meals.
organicManagement: Tolerate nests away from human activity; benefits as a biocontrol agent typically outweigh fruit damage in home gardens.
Remove problematic nests at night with a flashlight covered in red film and a long-handled scraper.
Bag ripe fruit clusters in mesh or paper bags 2 weeks before harvest.
Cover compost and food waste; wasps are strongly attracted to fermenting fruit.
entries.prevention: Inspect eaves and outbuildings in spring before nest construction. Avoid sweet beverages and open food in garden areas during late summer when colonies peak.

entries.symptoms: Adults 18 to 25 mm with black body and bold ivory-white markings on face, thorax, and abdomen tip. Build large football-shaped enclosed paper nests in trees, shrubs, and on building exteriors. Aggressive defenders of the nest. Important caterpillar and fly predators. Minor pest on ripe orchard fruit.
organicManagement: Tolerate nests well away from human activity; beneficial as caterpillar biocontrol.
Remove nests located near walkways and play areas at night when colonies are at rest; consult a professional for nests larger than a basketball.
Cover compost and outdoor food preparation areas in late summer.
entries.prevention: Inspect tree canopies and building eaves in late spring while nests are small. Maintain awareness of nest locations during outdoor work.

entries.symptoms: Adults 18 to 25 mm with black body and bold ivory-white markings on face, thorax, and abdomen tip. Build large football-shaped enclosed paper nests in trees, shrubs, and on building exteriors. Aggressive defenders of the nest. Important caterpillar and fly predators. Minor pest on ripe orchard fruit.
organicManagement: Tolerate nests well away from human activity; beneficial as caterpillar biocontrol.

entries.symptoms: Adults 12 to 16 mm with bright yellow and black banded abdomen and short antennae. Build large enclosed nests underground or in wall voids. Important caterpillar and fly predators during early to mid summer. Major orchard fruit pest in late summer: enter wounds opened by birds and other pests, expand them, and damage substantial portions of fruit. Common nuisance at outdoor meals.
organicManagement: Pick up windfall and over-ripe fruit promptly; do not let it accumulate.
Bag fruit clusters in mesh 2 weeks before harvest.
Trap with commercial heptyl butyrate yellowjacket traps placed at the orchard perimeter; provides only moderate suppression.
Tolerate underground nests well away from foot traffic; nests die out each fall in temperate regions.
entries.prevention: Maintain prompt fruit pickup throughout harvest. Cover compost and food waste. Mark underground nest entrances for professional removal if near walkways.

entries.symptoms: Light to medium gray-brown rabbit 35 to 45 cm long with white cotton-ball tail. Browses garden vegetables (lettuce, brassicas, beans, peas), strawberries, and tree bark in winter. Diagnostic: clean 45-degree cuts on browsed stems (rodent damage shows tooth chisels). Winter bark gnawing on young fruit trees can girdle and kill.
organicManagement: Hardware cloth or chicken wire fence at least 60 cm tall buried 15 cm deep around vegetable beds.
Tree guards (corrugated plastic or hardware cloth) on young fruit-tree trunks to 60 cm height for winter bark protection.
Repellents (predator urine, putrescent egg solids) require frequent reapplication and lose effectiveness over time.
Encourage natural predators (hawks, owls, foxes) by maintaining mature trees and avoiding rodenticides that move up the food chain.
entries.prevention: Permanent fencing is the only reliable long-term control. Eliminate brush piles and dense ground cover within 30 m of garden beds.

entries.symptoms: Gray squirrel 40 to 50 cm long including tail. Highly destructive on tomato (takes bites from multiple fruit without consuming any), sweet corn (peels back husks), sunflower heads, tree fruit, and nut crops. Strips bulbs and root vegetables. Climbs almost any barrier.
organicManagement: Hardware cloth cages over individual high-value tomato and sunflower plantings.
Bird netting over fruit trees, secured to the trunk; squirrels learn to climb over open netting.
Repellents (capsaicin sprays, predator urine) provide only short-term suppression.
Manage bird feeders within 30 m of vegetable beds, which serve as food anchors for squirrel populations.
entries.prevention: Remove bird feeders during fruit and vegetable peak season. Prune fruit tree limbs back from buildings and adjacent tall trees to deny climbing access.

entries.symptoms: Gray squirrel 40 to 50 cm long including tail. Highly destructive on tomato (takes bites from multiple fruit without consuming any), sweet corn (peels back husks), sunflower heads, tree fruit, and nut crops. Strips bulbs and root vegetables. Climbs almost any barrier.
organicManagement: Hardware cloth cages over individual high-value tomato and sunflower plantings.
Bird netting over fruit trees, secured to the trunk; squirrels learn to climb over open netting.
Repellents (capsaicin sprays, predator urine) provide only short-term suppression.
Manage bird feeders within 30 m of vegetable beds, which serve as food anchors for squirrel populations.
entries.prevention: Remove bird feeders during fruit and vegetable peak season. Prune fruit tree limbs back from buildings and adjacent tall trees to deny climbing access.

entries.symptoms: Heavy-bodied rodent 50 to 65 cm long with short legs and stubby tail. Excavate large multi-entrance burrows under sheds, decks, and stone walls. Browse vegetables (bean, broccoli, melon, lettuce) heavily, often clear-cutting entire rows in a single morning visit. Climb low fences and short trees for fruit and bark.
organicManagement: Heavy-duty fencing: 1.2 m welded wire fence with the top 30 cm bent outward to prevent climbing; bottom buried 30 cm deep with an outward L-shape to prevent digging.
Live trapping (Havahart) with cantaloupe or apple bait; check local regulations for relocation legality.
Sealing burrow entrances repeatedly forces relocation.
Encourage natural predators: red-tailed hawks, coyotes, and foxes in rural settings.
entries.prevention: Identify and seal abandoned burrows under outbuildings before they become attractive. Install fencing before damage starts; reactive fencing rarely deters established groundhogs.

entries.symptoms: Dark gray to silver fur, 12 to 18 cm long, with broad shovel-like front feet and minute eyes. Tunnel just below soil surface, raising visible ridges across lawn and garden beds. Insectivore (grubs, earthworms); does not eat plant material directly but disturbs root systems and provides tunnels for vole damage.
organicManagement: Tolerate moles in established lawns; they help aerate soil and consume Japanese beetle and June beetle grubs.
Trap with harpoon traps (Out O' Sight, Victor) placed in active surface runs; lethal but reliable.
Beneficial nematode soil treatment reduces grub populations, the primary mole food source; populations decline over 1 to 2 seasons.
Castor oil-based repellents (Mole-Med) provide moderate suppression in lawns; rarely effective in vegetable beds.
entries.prevention: Reduce grub populations in adjacent lawn through beneficial nematodes and maintaining lawn at 3 inches or taller. Hardware cloth bed liners (under raised beds) prevent mole tunneling into root crops.

entries.symptoms: Small (12 to 16 cm) gray-brown rodent with short tail and short ears nearly hidden in fur. Active year-round above and below snow. Diagnostic: girdling of fruit tree bark at and below snow line in winter; surface runways in lawn and orchard mulch. Severe winter girdling kills young fruit trees and blueberry bushes.
organicManagement: Hardware cloth tree guards (1.5 cm mesh) extending 60 cm above expected snow line on all young fruit trees.
Maintain a 1 m vegetation-free circle around fruit-tree trunks throughout the growing season.
Pull mulch back 15 cm from tree trunks in late fall; voles nest in mulch and damage extends through winter.
Encourage barn owls, hawks, and foxes; rodenticide use creates secondary poisoning hazards for natural predators.
entries.prevention: Annual fall sanitation: mow grass short, pull mulch back from trunks, install tree guards before snow. Trap monitoring with snap traps in active runways before winter damage.

entries.symptoms: Stout rodent 15 to 20 cm long with massive front claws, small eyes and ears, and external fur-lined cheek pouches. Diagnostic: crescent-shaped mounds of soil with a plug at one end, marking each surface entry to extensive tunnel systems. Feeds entirely below ground on roots and tubers; damages root crops, fruit-tree roots, and lawn turf.
organicManagement: Cinch traps (Macabee, Cinch) or box traps placed in main runways; the most reliable control method.
Hardware cloth bed liners under raised beds prevent gopher entry from below.
Flooding of tunnel systems for several hours forces gophers to surface; effective in heavy clay soils.
Encourage natural predators: barn owls, gopher snakes, hawks, and coyotes.
entries.prevention: Reduce alfalfa cover crops adjacent to vegetable beds; alfalfa supports very high gopher populations. Inspect and reset traps weekly during active periods.

entries.symptoms: Small (12 to 16 cm body) striped rodent with five dark and four light longitudinal stripes on the back. Damages bulb plantings (tulip, crocus), strawberries (takes single bites from many fruit), tomato fruit, and tree-fruit ripening on lower branches. Burrows under stone walls and patios.
organicManagement: Bird netting over strawberry beds; secure tightly to the ground.
Hardware cloth bulb baskets when planting tulips and crocus; bury below planting depth.
Live trapping with sunflower seed bait; check local regulations.
Tolerate moderate populations; chipmunks consume many garden pests (slugs, insect grubs).
entries.prevention: Reduce stone-wall and woodpile habitat within 30 m of high-value plantings. Plant daffodils (chipmunk-deterrent) alongside tulips to reduce predation of vulnerable bulbs.
